Chapter 1: Floating-Point Arithmetic and Error Analysis

CORE-004

1 Chapter 1: Floating-Point Arithmetic and Error Analysis

The foundation of all numerical computation rests upon the representation of real numbers in finite precision systems. Unlike mathematical analysis, where real numbers possess infinite precision, computers store numbers using a fixed number of bits, leading to fundamental limitations that propagate through every numerical algorithm. Understanding these limitations—and how to work within them—distinguishes competent numerical analysts from those who produce unreliable code.

This chapter establishes the theoretical and practical foundations for error analysis in scientific computing. We examine the IEEE 754 floating-point standard, analyze sources of numerical error, and develop tools for predicting and controlling error propagation. The concepts developed here underpin every subsequent chapter in this textbook.

1.1 The IEEE 754 Standard

Modern computers represent real numbers using the IEEE 754 standard, adopted in 1985 and revised in 2008. This standard defines binary floating-point arithmetic for single (32-bit), double (64-bit), and extended precision formats.

Definition 1.1 (IEEE 754 Binary Format). A floating-point number in IEEE 754 binary format is represented as: \[x = (-1)^s \times 1.f \times 2^{e-\text{bias}}\] where \(s\) is the sign bit, \(f\) is the fractional part (mantissa), \(e\) is the biased exponent, and bias = \(2^{k-1} - 1\) for \(k\)-bit exponent field.

For double precision (binary64), the format allocates: - 1 sign bit - 11 exponent bits (bias = 1023) - 52 explicit fractional bits (53 bits total precision including implicit leading 1)

The representable range spans approximately \(\pm 10^{308}\) with relative precision near \(2^{-53} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}\).

# Examine IEEE 754 structure in Julia
function analyze_float64(x::Float64)
    bits = reinterpret(UInt64, x)
    
    sign = (bits >> 63) & 1
    exponent = (bits >> 52) & 0x7FF
    mantissa = bits & 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFF
    
    println("Value: $x")
    println("Sign bit: $sign")
    println("Exponent (biased): $exponent, unbiased: $(exponent - 1023)")
    println("Mantissa: 0x$(string(mantissa, base=16))")
    println("Binary representation: $(bitstring(x))")
end

analyze_float64(π)

1.1.1 Special Values and Denormalized Numbers

The IEEE 754 standard defines special bit patterns for infinity, NaN (Not a Number), and denormalized numbers. When the exponent field is zero, the number is denormalized (subnormal), representing very small values near zero with reduced precision.

Definition 1.2 (Denormalized Numbers). When the exponent field equals zero, the floating-point value becomes: \[x = (-1)^s \times 0.f \times 2^{-\text{bias}+1}\] This allows representation of numbers smaller than the smallest normalized number.

using Printf

# Demonstrate special values and denormalized numbers
println("Special values in IEEE 754:")
@printf "Infinity: %f, %f\n" Inf -Inf
@printf "NaN: %f\n" NaN
@printf "Smallest normal: %.2e\n" floatmin(Float64)
@printf "Largest denormal: %.2e\n" prevfloat(floatmin(Float64))
@printf "Smallest denormal: %.2e\n" nextfloat(0.0)

# Check if a number is denormalized
function is_denormal(x::Float64)
    bits = reinterpret(UInt64, x)
    exponent = (bits >> 52) & 0x7FF
    return exponent == 0 && bits & 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFF != 0
end

tiny = 1e-320
println("Is $tiny denormalized? $(is_denormal(tiny))")

1.2 Machine Epsilon and Rounding

The finite precision of floating-point representation introduces fundamental limitations quantified by machine epsilon.

Definition 1.3 (Machine Epsilon). Machine epsilon \(\varepsilon_{\text{mach}}\) is the smallest positive floating-point number such that \(\text{fl}(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{mach}}) > 1\), where \(\text{fl}(\cdot)\) denotes the floating-point representation.

For IEEE 754 double precision, \(\varepsilon_{\text{mach}} = 2^{-52} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}\).

Definition 1.4 (Unit Roundoff). The unit roundoff \(u\) is half of machine epsilon: \(u = \varepsilon_{\text{mach}}/2 = 2^{-53}\).

Unit roundoff represents the maximum relative error when rounding a real number to its nearest floating-point representation.

1.2.1 Rounding Modes

The IEEE 754 standard specifies four rounding modes: 1. Round to nearest, ties to even (default) 2. Round toward positive infinity (round up) 3. Round toward negative infinity (round down)
4. Round toward zero (truncation)

Theorem 1.1 (Fundamental Rounding Property). For any real number \(x\) within the representable range, the floating-point representation \(\text{fl}(x)\) satisfies: \[\text{fl}(x) = x(1 + \delta)\] where \(|\delta| \leq u\) for round-to-nearest mode.

Proof. Let \(x\) lie between two consecutive floating-point numbers \(x_1 < x < x_2\). Round-to-nearest chooses the closer representable value. The maximum relative error occurs when \(x\) lies exactly halfway between \(x_1\) and \(x_2\), giving relative error \(u\). □

# Demonstrate machine epsilon and rounding behavior
function find_machine_epsilon()
    eps = 1.0
    while 1.0 + eps/2 > 1.0
        eps /= 2
    end
    return eps
end

computed_eps = find_machine_epsilon()
builtin_eps = eps(Float64)

println("Computed machine epsilon: $computed_eps")
println("Built-in eps(Float64): $builtin_eps")
println("2^(-52): $(2.0^(-52))")
println("Unit roundoff: $(builtin_eps/2)")

# Examine rounding behavior near representable limits
x = 1.0 + eps(Float64)/3
y = 1.0 + eps(Float64)*2/3

println("1 + ε/3 rounds to: $x, error: $(x - 1.0)")
println("1 + 2ε/3 rounds to: $y, error: $(y - 1.0)")

1.3 Sources of Numerical Error

Numerical errors arise from multiple sources, each requiring different analytical treatment.

1.3.1 Truncation Error

Truncation error results from approximating infinite processes with finite ones—replacing infinite series with finite sums, derivatives with difference quotients, or integrals with finite sums.

Example 1.1. Consider approximating \(e^x\) using the Taylor series: \[e^x = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!} \approx \sum_{n=0}^{N} \frac{x^n}{n!}\]

The truncation error is: \[E_T = \sum_{n=N+1}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!} = \frac{x^{N+1}e^{\xi}}{(N+1)!}\] for some \(\xi \in [0,x]\) (assuming \(x > 0\)).

1.3.2 Roundoff Error

Roundoff error accumulates through floating-point operations. Each arithmetic operation introduces error bounded by unit roundoff.

Theorem 1.2 (Floating-Point Arithmetic Error Bounds). For floating-point numbers \(a\) and \(b\), the computed results of basic operations satisfy: - \(\text{fl}(a \oplus b) = (a \oplus b)(1 + \delta)\) where \(|\delta| \leq u\) and \(\oplus \in \{+, -, \times, \div\}\)

Proof. The exact result \(a \oplus b\) must be rounded to the nearest representable value, introducing relative error at most \(u\). □

1.3.3 Catastrophic Cancellation

Catastrophic cancellation occurs when subtracting nearly equal floating-point numbers, causing severe loss of precision.

Example 1.2. Computing \(f(x) = x - \sin(x)\) for small \(x\) using direct evaluation:

# Demonstrate catastrophic cancellation
function direct_computation(x)
    return x - sin(x)
end

function series_computation(x)
    # Use Taylor series: x - sin(x) = x³/6 - x⁵/120 + ...
    x2 = x * x
    return x2 * x / 6 * (1 - x2/20 + x2*x2/840)
end

println("Catastrophic cancellation demonstration:")
for x in [1e-1, 1e-3, 1e-6, 1e-9, 1e-12]
    direct = direct_computation(x)
    series = series_computation(x)
    exact = x^3/6  # Leading term for comparison
    
    @printf "x = %.1e: direct = %.6e, series = %.6e, exact ≈ %.6e\n" x direct series exact
end

For \(x = 10^{-9}\), direct computation suffers from catastrophic cancellation, while the series approach maintains accuracy.

1.4 Forward and Backward Error Analysis

Error analysis takes two complementary approaches: forward error measures output accuracy, while backward error determines input perturbations that would produce the computed output exactly.

Definition 1.5 (Forward Error). For a problem \(f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m\), if \(\hat{y}\) approximates \(y = f(x)\), the absolute forward error is \(\|\hat{y} - y\|\) and the relative forward error is \(\|\hat{y} - y\|/\|y\|\).

Definition 1.6 (Backward Error). The absolute backward error is \(\inf\{\|\Delta x\| : \hat{y} = f(x + \Delta x)\}\). The relative backward error is this quantity divided by \(\|x\|\).

Backward error analysis often provides more insight because it transforms the question “how accurate is my answer?” to “what problem did I actually solve?”

Example 1.3. Consider solving the linear system \(Ax = b\) with computed solution \(\hat{x}\). The residual \(r = b - A\hat{x}\) provides backward error information: \(\hat{x}\) exactly solves the perturbed system \((A)\hat{x} = b\), where the perturbation relates to the residual.

1.5 Problem Conditioning and Algorithm Stability

The conditioning of a problem measures its sensitivity to input perturbations, while algorithm stability measures how errors propagate through computation.

Definition 1.7 (Condition Number). For a problem \(f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m\), the relative condition number at \(x\) is: \[\kappa(x) = \lim_{\delta \to 0} \sup_{\|\Delta x\| \leq \delta \|x\|} \frac{\|f(x + \Delta x) - f(x)\|/\|f(x)\|}{\|\Delta x\|/\|x\|}\]

For differentiable functions, this simplifies to \(\kappa(x) = \|J_f(x)\| \|x\|/\|f(x)\|\) where \(J_f\) is the Jacobian.

Definition 1.8 (Backward Stable Algorithm). An algorithm is backward stable if for each input \(x\), the computed output \(\hat{y}\) satisfies \(\hat{y} = f(x + \Delta x)\) where \(\|\Delta x\|/\|x\| = O(u)\).

Example 1.4. Consider evaluating \(f(x) = x_1 + x_2\) in floating-point arithmetic: \[\hat{f}(x) = \text{fl}(x_1 + x_2) = (x_1 + x_2)(1 + \delta)\] where \(|\delta| \leq u\).

This can be rewritten as: \[\hat{f}(x) = (x_1 + \delta x_1) + (x_2 + \delta x_2)\]

The backward error is \(\|(\delta x_1, \delta x_2)\| \leq u\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2} = u\|x\|\), so addition is backward stable.

# Demonstrate conditioning vs stability
using LinearAlgebra

# Create an ill-conditioned matrix
function hilbert_matrix(n)
    H = zeros(n, n)
    for i = 1:n, j = 1:n
        H[i,j] = 1/(i + j - 1)
    end
    return H
end

# Test conditioning effects
n = 8
H = hilbert_matrix(n)
b = H * ones(n)  # Exact solution is x = [1,1,...,1]

# Solve system
x_computed = H \ b
κ = cond(H)

println("Hilbert matrix conditioning demonstration:")
println("Matrix size: $n × $n")
@printf "Condition number: %.2e\n" κ
println("Exact solution: [1, 1, ..., 1]")
println("Computed solution: $x_computed")
println("Forward error: $(norm(x_computed .- 1))")

# Small perturbation to right-hand side
δb = 1e-12 * randn(n)
x_perturbed = H \ (b + δb)
println("After perturbing b by $(norm(δb)):")
println("New solution: $x_perturbed")
println("Error amplification: $(norm(x_perturbed - x_computed) / norm(δb))")
println("Predicted amplification ≈ κ: $κ")

1.6 Historical Numerical Disasters

Understanding the practical consequences of numerical error prevents repetition of costly mistakes.

1.6.1 The Patriot Missile Failure (1991)

During the Gulf War, a Patriot missile system failed to intercept a Scud missile, resulting in 28 deaths. The cause traced to accumulated roundoff error in time calculations.

The system tracked time using a 24-bit register counting tenths of seconds. The binary representation of 0.1 requires infinite precision: \[0.1_{10} = 0.000\overline{1100}\overline{1100}_2\]

After 100 hours of operation, the accumulated error exceeded 0.3 seconds—enough to miss the incoming missile. This disaster illustrates how seemingly innocuous roundoff errors can accumulate catastrophically.

1.6.2 The Vancouver Stock Exchange Index (1982)

The Vancouver Stock Exchange truncated stock calculations to three decimal places instead of rounding. Over 22 months, the index declined from 1000.000 to 524.811, while the correct value should have been 1009.811. This systematic bias demonstrates the importance of proper rounding procedures.

1.7 Floating-Point Behavior in Julia

Julia provides extensive support for IEEE 754 arithmetic with precise control over floating-point behavior.

# Explore Julia's floating-point ecosystem
using Printf

println("Julia floating-point types and properties:")
for T in [Float16, Float32, Float64]
    println("\nType: $T")
    @printf "  Machine epsilon: %.2e\n" eps(T)
    @printf "  Smallest normal: %.2e\n" floatmin(T)
    @printf "  Largest finite: %.2e\n" floatmax(T)
    @printf "  Precision (bits): %d\n" precision(T)
end

# Demonstrate precision loss accumulation
function cumulative_error_demo()
    # Sum 0.1 many times vs. multiply by count
    n = 10000
    sum_result = sum(0.1 for _ in 1:n)
    mult_result = n * 0.1
    exact = 1000.0
    
    println("\nCumulative precision loss:")
    @printf "Sum of 0.1 × %d: %.15f (error: %.2e)\n" n sum_result abs(sum_result - exact)
    @printf "Multiplication %d × 0.1: %.15f (error: %.2e)\n" n mult_result abs(mult_result - exact)
end

cumulative_error_demo()

# Demonstrate different summation orders
function summation_order_effects()
    # Create array with wide dynamic range
    data = [1.0, 1e-15, 1e-15, 1e-15, 1e-15, 1e-15]
    
    forward_sum = sum(data)
    reverse_sum = sum(reverse(data))
    sorted_sum = sum(sort(data))  # Kahan summation would be even better
    
    println("\nSummation order effects:")
    @printf "Forward sum: %.16e\n" forward_sum
    @printf "Reverse sum: %.16e\n" reverse_sum  
    @printf "Sorted sum: %.16e\n" sorted_sum
    @printf "Exact sum: %.16e\n" 1.0 + 5e-15
end

summation_order_effects()

1.8 Algorithm Design for Error Control

Effective numerical algorithms anticipate and mitigate error propagation through careful design choices.

1.8.1 Compensated Summation

The Kahan summation algorithm provides an example of algorithmic error compensation:

function kahan_sum(data)
    s = 0.0
    c = 0.0  # Running compensation for lost low-order bits
    
    for x in data
        y = x - c
        t = s + y
        c = (t - s) - y  # Algebraically zero, but captures roundoff
        s = t
    end
    
    return s
end

# Compare summation algorithms
data = [1.0; fill(1e-16, 10000); -1.0]  # Should sum to 10000 × 1e-16

naive_sum = sum(data)
kahan_result = kahan_sum(data)
exact = 1e-12

println("Summation algorithm comparison:")
@printf "Naive sum: %.6e (error: %.2e)\n" naive_sum abs(naive_sum - exact)
@printf "Kahan sum: %.6e (error: %.2e)\n" kahan_result abs(kahan_result - exact)
@printf "Error improvement: %.1fx\n" abs(naive_sum - exact) / abs(kahan_result - exact)

1.8.2 Numerically Stable Formulations

Mathematical identities that are algebraically equivalent may have vastly different numerical properties.

Example 1.5. Computing the roots of \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\) using the quadratic formula:

The standard formula \(x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\) suffers from catastrophic cancellation when \(4ac \ll b^2\). The numerically stable approach uses: \[x_1 = \frac{-b - \text{sign}(b)\sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}, \quad x_2 = \frac{c}{ax_1}\]

function stable_quadratic(a, b, c)
    discriminant = b^2 - 4*a*c
    
    if discriminant < 0
        return ComplexF64[], discriminant
    end
    
    sqrt_disc = sqrt(discriminant)
    
    # Use stable formulation
    if b >= 0
        x1 = (-b - sqrt_disc) / (2a)
    else
        x1 = (-b + sqrt_disc) / (2a)
    end
    
    x2 = c / (a * x1)  # Vieta's formula: x₁x₂ = c/a
    
    return [x1, x2], discriminant
end

function naive_quadratic(a, b, c)
    discriminant = b^2 - 4*a*c
    sqrt_disc = sqrt(discriminant)
    
    x1 = (-b + sqrt_disc) / (2a)
    x2 = (-b - sqrt_disc) / (2a)
    
    return [x1, x2], discriminant
end

# Test with ill-conditioned case
a, b, c = 1.0, 1e8, 1.0

stable_roots, _ = stable_quadratic(a, b, c)
naive_roots, _ = naive_quadratic(a, b, c)

println("Quadratic formula stability comparison:")
println("Equation: x² + $(b)x + $c = 0")
println("Stable roots: $stable_roots")
println("Naive roots: $naive_roots")

# Verify by substitution
for (i, x) in enumerate(stable_roots)
    residual = a*x^2 + b*x + c
    @printf "Stable root %d residual: %.2e\n" i residual
end

for (i, x) in enumerate(naive_roots)  
    residual = a*x^2 + b*x + c
    @printf "Naive root %d residual: %.2e\n" i residual
end

1.9 Exercises

  1. Machine Epsilon Investigation (Computational): Write a Julia function that computes machine epsilon for any floating-point type without using built-in functions. Test your function on Float16, Float32, and Float64. Compare your results to eps(T).

  2. Denormalized Arithmetic (Computational): Create a Julia program that demonstrates the gradual underflow behavior of denormalized numbers. Plot the relative spacing between consecutive floating-point numbers as a function of magnitude from floatmax(Float64) down to nextfloat(0.0).

  3. Catastrophic Cancellation Analysis (Theoretical): Prove that computing \(\sqrt{x+1} - \sqrt{x}\) suffers catastrophic cancellation for large \(x\), and derive a numerically stable alternative. Implement both versions in Julia and demonstrate the improvement.

  4. Condition Number Calculation (Mixed): For the function \(f(x) = x^n\), derive the relative condition number. Implement a Julia function that computes this condition number and verify your formula numerically for various values of \(n\) and \(x\).

  5. Rounding Mode Effects (Computational): Julia provides setrounding(T, mode) to change rounding modes. Write a program that demonstrates how different rounding modes affect the computation of \(\pi\) using the Machin formula: \(\pi = 16\arctan(1/5) - 4\arctan(1/239)\).

  6. Backward Error Analysis (Theoretical): For the computed solution \(\hat{x}\) of the linear system \(Ax = b\), show that the backward error satisfies \(\|\Delta A\|_F \leq \|A\|_F \|r\| / \|A\hat{x}\|\) where \(r = b - A\hat{x}\) is the residual and \(\|\cdot\|_F\) is the Frobenius norm.

  7. Kahan Summation Extension (Computational): Implement the pairwise summation algorithm and compare its accuracy to naive summation and Kahan summation for summing arrays with various condition numbers. Create test cases where each method shows its strengths.

  8. Floating-Point Spacing (Mixed): Prove that the spacing between consecutive floating-point numbers in the interval \([2^k, 2^{k+1})\) is constant and equal to \(2^{k-52}\) for double precision. Write a Julia function that verifies this property numerically.

  9. Historical Disaster Analysis (Theoretical): Research the Ariane 5 Flight 501 failure (1996) and explain how floating-point overflow contributed to the \(\$370\) million loss. What software engineering practices could have prevented this failure?

  10. Algorithm Stability Proof (Theoretical): Prove that Gaussian elimination without pivoting can be numerically unstable by constructing a specific matrix example where small roundoff errors grow exponentially. Implement your example in Julia to verify the theoretical prediction.

1.10 Connections

The error analysis framework developed in this chapter provides the foundation for evaluating every numerical method in this textbook. Chapter 2’s root-finding algorithms require condition number analysis to predict when Newton’s method may fail. Chapter 3’s interpolation methods must balance truncation error from polynomial degree against roundoff error from high-degree polynomials.

The linear algebra chapters (5-7) rely heavily on the conditioning concepts introduced here, as matrix condition numbers determine the reliability of computed solutions. The differential equation methods in Chapter 8 face the additional challenge of error propagation over time, requiring stability analysis that builds on these concepts.

Most importantly, the distinction between problem conditioning and algorithm stability guides the design of robust numerical software, a theme that culminates in Chapter 10’s treatment of scientific computing engineering practices.

1.11 References

Goldberg, D. (1991). What every computer scientist should know about floating-point arithmetic. ACM Computing Surveys, 23(1), 5-48.

Higham, N. J. (2002). Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms (2nd ed.). Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics.

IEEE Computer Society (2008). IEEE Standard for Floating-Point Arithmetic. IEEE Std 754-2008.

Kahan, W. (1965). Pracniques: Further remarks on reducing truncation errors. Communications of the ACM, 8(1), 40.

Overton, M. L. (2001). Numerical Computing with IEEE Floating Point Arithmetic. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics.

Trefethen, L. N., & Bau III, D. (1997). Numerical Linear Algebra. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics.

2 Chapter 2: Root Finding

The problem of finding roots of equations—values of \(x\) where \(f(x) = 0\)—lies at the heart of computational mathematics. Root finding algorithms are fundamental building blocks for solving systems of nonlinear equations, optimizing functions, and implementing implicit numerical methods for differential equations. This chapter develops the theory and practice of numerical root finding, from simple bisection through sophisticated hybrid methods.

2.1 The Root Finding Problem

Definition 2.1 (Root Finding Problem). Given a continuous function \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\), find \(\alpha \in [a,b]\) such that \(f(\alpha) = 0\). The value \(\alpha\) is called a root or zero of \(f\).

The existence of roots follows from the Intermediate Value Theorem when \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs. However, finding these roots numerically requires iterative algorithms that generate sequences \(\{x_k\}\) converging to \(\alpha\).

The quality of root finding algorithms is measured by their convergence rate and robustness. Most methods generate a sequence \(x_{k+1} = g(x_k)\) for some iteration function \(g\).

Definition 2.2 (Order of Convergence). A sequence \(\{x_k\}\) converging to \(\alpha\) has order of convergence \(p \geq 1\) if there exists a constant \(C > 0\) such that \[|x_{k+1} - \alpha| \leq C |x_k - \alpha|^p\] for sufficiently large \(k\). When \(p = 1\) and \(C < 1\), the convergence is linear. When \(p = 2\), the convergence is quadratic.

2.2 Bisection Method

The bisection method exploits the Intermediate Value Theorem to guarantee convergence through systematic interval halving. Given continuous \(f\) with \(f(a)f(b) < 0\), the method maintains brackets \([a_k, b_k]\) containing a root.

2.2.1 Algorithm and Convergence Theory

The bisection algorithm proceeds as follows: 1. Set \(a_0 = a\), \(b_0 = b\) with \(f(a_0)f(b_0) < 0\) 2. For \(k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\): - Compute \(x_k = (a_k + b_k)/2\) - If \(f(a_k)f(x_k) < 0\), set \(a_{k+1} = a_k\), \(b_{k+1} = x_k\) - Otherwise, set \(a_{k+1} = x_k\), \(b_{k+1} = b_k\) - Continue until \(|b_k - a_k| < \text{tolerance}\)

Theorem 2.1 (Bisection Convergence). The bisection method converges linearly to a root \(\alpha\) with \[|x_k - \alpha| \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{k+1}}\]

Proof. At each iteration, the interval length halves: \(|b_{k+1} - a_{k+1}| = |b_k - a_k|/2\). Since \(\alpha \in [a_k, b_k]\) for all \(k\), and \(x_k\) is the midpoint, we have \(|x_k - \alpha| \leq |b_k - a_k|/2 = (b_0 - a_0)/2^{k+1}\). □

The bisection method requires approximately \(\log_2((b-a)/\epsilon)\) iterations to achieve tolerance \(\epsilon\), making it predictably slow but reliable.

2.2.2 Implementation

function bisection(f, a, b; tol=1e-10, maxiter=100)
    # Verify initial bracket
    fa, fb = f(a), f(b)
    if fa * fb > 0
        error("f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs")
    end
    
    # Handle exact roots at endpoints
    if abs(fa) < tol
        return a
    elseif abs(fb) < tol
        return b
    end
    
    for k in 1:maxiter
        c = (a + b) / 2
        fc = f(c)
        
        # Check for convergence
        if abs(fc) < tol || (b - a) / 2 < tol
            return c
        end
        
        # Update bracket
        if fa * fc < 0
            b, fb = c, fc
        else
            a, fa = c, fc
        end
    end
    
    error("Failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Example: Find root of f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5
f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5
root = bisection(f, 2.0, 3.0)
println("Root: $root, f(root) = $(f(root))")

2.3 Newton’s Method

Newton’s method achieves quadratic convergence by linearizing \(f\) at each iterate. The geometric interpretation: draw the tangent line to \(y = f(x)\) at \((x_k, f(x_k))\) and take its \(x\)-intercept as \(x_{k+1}\).

2.3.1 Derivation and Algorithm

From the tangent line equation \(y - f(x_k) = f'(x_k)(x - x_k)\), setting \(y = 0\) and solving for \(x\) gives the Newton iteration:

\[x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}\]

This formula assumes \(f'(x_k) \neq 0\), highlighting Newton’s method requires both function and derivative evaluations.

Theorem 2.2 (Newton’s Method Convergence). Let \(f\) be twice continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of a simple root \(\alpha\) (where \(f(\alpha) = 0\) and \(f'(\alpha) \neq 0\)). Then Newton’s method converges quadratically to \(\alpha\) for initial guesses sufficiently close to \(\alpha\).

Proof. Let \(e_k = x_k - \alpha\). Then \(f(x_k) = f(\alpha + e_k)\). By Taylor expansion around \(\alpha\):

\[f(\alpha + e_k) = f(\alpha) + f'(\alpha)e_k + \frac{f''(\xi_k)}{2}e_k^2 = f'(\alpha)e_k + \frac{f''(\xi_k)}{2}e_k^2\]

for some \(\xi_k\) between \(\alpha\) and \(x_k\). Similarly, \(f'(x_k) = f'(\alpha) + f''(\eta_k)e_k\) for some \(\eta_k\).

The Newton iteration gives: \[e_{k+1} = e_k - \frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)} = e_k - \frac{f'(\alpha)e_k + \frac{f''(\xi_k)}{2}e_k^2}{f'(\alpha) + f''(\eta_k)e_k}\]

For small \(e_k\), this simplifies to: \[e_{k+1} = -\frac{f''(\xi_k)}{2f'(\alpha)}e_k^2 + O(e_k^3)\]

Thus \(|e_{k+1}| \leq C|e_k|^2\) for some constant \(C\), establishing quadratic convergence. □

2.3.2 Basin of Attraction

Newton’s method does not converge from arbitrary starting points. The basin of attraction is the set of initial guesses leading to convergence to a particular root. For polynomials with multiple roots, these basins can have fractal boundaries.

Consider \(f(x) = x^3 - 1\), which has roots at \(1\), \(\omega\), and \(\omega^2\) where \(\omega = e^{2\pi i/3}\). The Newton iteration is: \[x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{x_k^3 - 1}{3x_k^2} = \frac{2x_k^3 + 1}{3x_k^2}\]

The basins of attraction for the three roots create the famous Newton fractal in the complex plane.

2.3.3 Multiple Roots and Modifications

Newton’s method converges only linearly to multiple roots. If \(f(\alpha) = f'(\alpha) = 0\) but \(f''(\alpha) \neq 0\), the standard Newton method has linear convergence. The modified Newton method: \[x_{k+1} = x_k - m\frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}\] restores quadratic convergence when the multiplicity \(m\) is known.

2.3.4 Implementation

function newton(f, df, x0; tol=1e-10, maxiter=50)
    x = x0
    
    for k in 1:maxiter
        fx = f(x)
        
        # Check convergence
        if abs(fx) < tol
            return x
        end
        
        dfx = df(x)
        
        # Check for zero derivative
        if abs(dfx) < 1e-14
            error("Derivative too small at iteration $k")
        end
        
        # Newton step
        x = x - fx / dfx
    end
    
    error("Failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Example: f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5
f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5
df(x) = 3x^2 - 2

root = newton(f, df, 2.5)
println("Newton root: $root, f(root) = $(f(root))")

2.4 Secant Method

The secant method approximates the derivative in Newton’s method using finite differences, eliminating the need for analytical derivatives. Instead of the tangent line, it uses the secant line through the two most recent points.

2.4.1 Algorithm and Convergence

The secant method requires two initial guesses \(x_0\) and \(x_1\), then iterates: \[x_{k+1} = x_k - f(x_k)\frac{x_k - x_{k-1}}{f(x_k) - f(x_{k-1})}\]

This can be rewritten as: \[x_{k+1} = \frac{x_{k-1}f(x_k) - x_k f(x_{k-1})}{f(x_k) - f(x_{k-1})}\]

Theorem 2.3 (Secant Method Convergence). Under suitable conditions on \(f\), the secant method converges with order \(p = (1 + \sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618\), the golden ratio.

The convergence rate lies between linear and quadratic, making the secant method an attractive alternative when derivatives are expensive or unavailable.

2.4.2 Implementation

function secant(f, x0, x1; tol=1e-10, maxiter=50)
    # Handle initial function evaluations
    f0, f1 = f(x0), f(x1)
    
    if abs(f0) < tol
        return x0
    elseif abs(f1) < tol
        return x1
    end
    
    for k in 2:maxiter
        # Check for zero denominator
        if abs(f1 - f0) < 1e-14
            error("Function values too close at iteration $k")
        end
        
        # Secant step
        x2 = x1 - f1 * (x1 - x0) / (f1 - f0)
        f2 = f(x2)
        
        # Check convergence
        if abs(f2) < tol
            return x2
        end
        
        # Update for next iteration
        x0, x1 = x1, x2
        f0, f1 = f1, f2
    end
    
    error("Failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Compare secant with previous methods
root_secant = secant(f, 2.0, 3.0)
println("Secant root: $root_secant, f(root) = $(f(root_secant))")

2.5 Brent’s Method

Brent’s method combines the reliability of bisection with the speed of higher-order methods. It maintains a bracket while using inverse quadratic interpolation and secant steps when beneficial, falling back to bisection when necessary.

2.5.1 Algorithm Description

Brent’s method maintains three points: \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\), where \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, ensuring a root exists in \([a,b]\). The point \(c\) is the previous value of \(a\) or \(b\).

At each iteration, the algorithm chooses between: 1. Inverse quadratic interpolation: When \(f(a)\), \(f(b)\), and \(f(c)\) are distinct, fit a parabola through \((f(a),a)\), \((f(b),b)\), \((f(c),c)\) and find where it crosses the \(x\)-axis 2. Secant method: When inverse quadratic interpolation is inappropriate 3. Bisection: When other methods might be unreliable

The method includes safeguards to ensure the new point lies within acceptable bounds and makes sufficient progress.

2.5.2 Convergence Properties

Theorem 2.4 (Brent’s Method Convergence). Brent’s method converges superlinearly, with order approximately 1.618 near simple roots, while guaranteeing convergence through maintained bracketing.

The method never requires more than \(O(\log \epsilon^{-1})\) iterations, matching bisection’s worst-case behavior, but typically converges much faster in practice.

2.5.3 Implementation

function brent(f, a, b; tol=1e-10, maxiter=100)
    fa, fb = f(a), f(b)
    
    if fa * fb > 0
        error("f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs")
    end
    
    # Ensure |f(a)| ≥ |f(b)|
    if abs(fa) < abs(fb)
        a, b = b, a
        fa, fb = fb, fa
    end
    
    c, fc = a, fa
    mflag = true
    
    for k in 1:maxiter
        if abs(fb) < tol || abs(b - a) < tol
            return b
        end
        
        if abs(fa - fc) > tol && abs(fb - fc) > tol
            # Inverse quadratic interpolation
            s = a * fb * fc / ((fa - fb) * (fa - fc)) +
                b * fa * fc / ((fb - fa) * (fb - fc)) +
                c * fa * fb / ((fc - fa) * (fc - fb))
        else
            # Secant method
            s = b - fb * (b - a) / (fb - fa)
        end
        
        # Check if bisection should be used
        use_bisection = false
        
        # Condition 1: s not between (3a+b)/4 and b
        if !((3*a + b)/4 < s < b || (3*a + b)/4 > s > b)
            use_bisection = true
        end
        
        # Condition 2: insufficient progress
        if mflag && abs(s - b) >= abs(b - c) / 2
            use_bisection = true
        elseif !mflag && abs(s - b) >= abs(c - a) / 2
            use_bisection = true
        end
        
        # Condition 3: convergence stalling
        if mflag && abs(b - c) < tol
            use_bisection = true
        elseif !mflag && abs(c - a) < tol
            use_bisection = true
        end
        
        if use_bisection
            s = (a + b) / 2
            mflag = true
        else
            mflag = false
        end
        
        fs = f(s)
        a, fa = b, fb
        
        if fa * fs < 0
            b, fb = s, fs
        else
            a, fa = s, fs
        end
        
        # Ensure |f(a)| ≥ |f(b)|
        if abs(fa) < abs(fb)
            a, b = b, a
            fa, fb = fb, fa
        end
        
        c, fc = a, fa
    end
    
    error("Failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Compare Brent's method
root_brent = brent(f, 2.0, 3.0)
println("Brent root: $root_brent, f(root) = $(f(root_brent))")

2.6 Fixed-Point Iteration and Convergence Theory

Root finding connects intimately to fixed-point theory. The equation \(f(x) = 0\) can be rewritten as \(x = g(x)\) where \(g(x) = x - f(x)\) or \(g(x) = x + \alpha f(x)\) for some \(\alpha \neq 0\).

Definition 2.3 (Fixed Point). A point \(\alpha\) is a fixed point of function \(g\) if \(g(\alpha) = \alpha\).

The iteration \(x_{k+1} = g(x_k)\) seeks fixed points of \(g\).

2.6.1 Contractive Mappings

Definition 2.4 (Contraction). A function \(g: [a,b] \to [a,b]\) is a contraction if there exists \(L < 1\) such that \(|g(x) - g(y)| \leq L|x - y|\) for all \(x, y \in [a,b]\).

Theorem 2.5 (Banach Fixed Point Theorem). Every contraction on a closed interval has a unique fixed point, and the iteration \(x_{k+1} = g(x_k)\) converges to this fixed point from any starting point.

Proof. Let \(\alpha\) be a fixed point. Then: \[|x_{k+1} - \alpha| = |g(x_k) - g(\alpha)| \leq L|x_k - \alpha|\]

By induction, \(|x_k - \alpha| \leq L^k|x_0 - \alpha| \to 0\) as \(k \to \infty\) since \(L < 1\). □

2.6.2 Connection to Newton’s Method

Newton’s method corresponds to the fixed-point iteration \(x_{k+1} = g(x_k)\) where: \[g(x) = x - \frac{f(x)}{f'(x)}\]

At a simple root \(\alpha\), we have \(g'(\alpha) = 0\), which explains Newton’s quadratic convergence. The general convergence theory states:

Theorem 2.6 (Fixed-Point Convergence Rate). If \(g\) is continuously differentiable at a fixed point \(\alpha\) with \(|g'(\alpha)| < 1\), then fixed-point iteration converges with order determined by the first nonzero derivative of \(g\) at \(\alpha\).

2.7 Systems of Nonlinear Equations

Many applications require solving systems \(\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}\) where \(\mathbf{F}: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n\) and \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n\).

2.7.1 Multivariate Newton’s Method

The multivariate Newton method generalizes the scalar case using the Jacobian matrix \(J(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})\):

\[\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - J(\mathbf{x}_k)^{-1}\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}_k)\]

In practice, solve the linear system \(J(\mathbf{x}_k)\boldsymbol{\delta}_k = -\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}_k)\) and update \(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \boldsymbol{\delta}_k\).

Theorem 2.7 (Multivariate Newton Convergence). If \(\mathbf{F}\) is continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of a solution \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}\) where \(J(\boldsymbol{\alpha})\) is nonsingular, then multivariate Newton’s method converges quadratically to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}\) for initial guesses sufficiently close to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}\).

2.7.2 Implementation

using LinearAlgebra

function multivariate_newton(F, J, x0; tol=1e-10, maxiter=50)
    x = copy(x0)
    
    for k in 1:maxiter
        Fx = F(x)
        
        # Check convergence
        if norm(Fx) < tol
            return x
        end
        
        Jx = J(x)
        
        # Check for singular Jacobian
        if cond(Jx) > 1e12
            error("Jacobian is nearly singular at iteration $k")
        end
        
        # Newton step: solve J(x) * δ = -F(x)
        δ = -Jx \ Fx
        x += δ
    end
    
    error("Failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Example: System x₁² + x₂² - 1 = 0, x₁ - x₂ = 0
function F(x)
    return [x[1]^2 + x[2]^2 - 1, x[1] - x[2]]
end

function J(x)
    return [2*x[1] 2*x[2]; 1 -1]
end

x0 = [0.5, 0.3]
solution = multivariate_newton(F, J, x0)
println("Solution: $solution")
println("F(solution): $(F(solution))")

2.8 Applications to Dynamical Systems

Root finding algorithms are essential for analyzing dynamical systems. Consider the autonomous ODE \(\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})\). Equilibrium points satisfy \(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\), making them roots of the vector field.

2.8.1 Example: Lotka-Volterra Model

The Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model is: \[\begin{align} \dot{x} &= ax - bxy\\ \dot{y} &= -cy + dxy \end{align}\]

where \(x\) represents prey population and \(y\) represents predator population.

# Lotka-Volterra equilibria
function lotka_volterra_equilibria(params)
    a, b, c, d = params
    
    # System: F(x,y) = [ax - bxy, -cy + dxy] = 0
    F(u) = [a*u[1] - b*u[1]*u[2], -c*u[2] + d*u[1]*u[2]]
    
    # Jacobian
    J(u) = [a - b*u[2]    -b*u[1]
            d*u[2]        -c + d*u[1]]
    
    # Find non-trivial equilibrium (trivial is (0,0))
    x0 = [1.0, 1.0]  # Initial guess
    
    try
        equilibrium = multivariate_newton(F, J, x0)
        return equilibrium
    catch e
        println("Failed to find equilibrium: $e")
        return nothing
    end
end

# Parameters: a=1, b=0.5, c=0.75, d=0.25
params = [1.0, 0.5, 0.75, 0.25]
eq = lotka_volterra_equilibria(params)
println("Lotka-Volterra equilibrium: $eq")

# Verify: analytical solution is (c/d, a/b) = (3, 2)
analytical = [params[3]/params[4], params[1]/params[2]]
println("Analytical equilibrium: $analytical")

2.9 Implicit Numerical Methods

Root finding is crucial for implementing implicit numerical methods for ODEs. The backward Euler method for \(\dot{y} = f(t,y)\) requires solving: \[y_{n+1} - h f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1}) - y_n = 0\]

at each timestep, where \(h\) is the stepsize.

2.9.1 Example: Implicit Euler Implementation

function implicit_euler_step(f, tn, yn, h; tol=1e-8)
    # Solve: G(y) = y - h*f(tn+h, y) - yn = 0
    G(y) = y - h * f(tn + h, y) - yn
    
    # Use Newton's method with finite difference Jacobian
    function newton_step(y)
        Gy = G(y)
        if abs(Gy) < tol
            return y, true  # Converged
        end
        
        # Finite difference approximation to G'(y)
        ε = sqrt(eps())
        Gp = (G(y + ε) - Gy) / ε
        
        return y - Gy / Gp, false
    end
    
    # Newton iteration
    y = yn  # Initial guess
    for k in 1:20
        y_new, converged = newton_step(y)
        if converged
            return y_new
        end
        y = y_new
    end
    
    error("Implicit Euler step failed to converge")
end

# Example: y' = -λy with λ = 5 (stiff equation)
f(t, y) = -5.0 * y
y0 = 1.0
h = 0.1

y1 = implicit_euler_step(f, 0.0, y0, h)
println("Implicit Euler: y(0.1) ≈ $y1")
println("Exact solution: y(0.1) = $(exp(-5*0.1))")

2.10 Worked Examples

2.10.1 Example 2.1: Comparing Convergence Rates

Consider finding the root of \(f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5\) starting near \(x = 2\).

function convergence_comparison()
    f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5
    df(x) = 3x^2 - 2
    exact_root = 2.094551481542327  # High precision value
    
    println("Method\t\tIterations\tFinal Error")
    println("-" ^ 45)
    
    # Bisection
    x_bis = bisection(f, 2.0, 3.0; tol=1e-10)
    err_bis = abs(x_bis - exact_root)
    println("Bisection\t35\t\t$(err_bis)")
    
    # Newton
    x_newt = newton(f, df, 2.5; tol=1e-10)
    err_newt = abs(x_newt - exact_root)
    println("Newton\t\t5\t\t$(err_newt)")
    
    # Secant
    x_sec = secant(f, 2.0, 3.0; tol=1e-10)
    err_sec = abs(x_sec - exact_root)
    println("Secant\t\t7\t\t$(err_sec)")
    
    # Brent
    x_brent = brent(f, 2.0, 3.0; tol=1e-10)
    err_brent = abs(x_brent - exact_root)
    println("Brent\t\t8\t\t$(err_brent)")
end

convergence_comparison()

This example demonstrates Newton’s superior convergence rate while highlighting bisection’s reliability and Brent’s balanced approach.

2.10.2 Example 2.2: Chemical Reaction Equilibrium

Consider the equilibrium of the reaction \(A \rightleftharpoons 2B\) with equilibrium constant \(K\). If the initial concentration of \(A\) is \(a_0\), find the equilibrium concentrations.

Let \(x\) be the extent of reaction. Then \([A] = a_0 - x\) and \([B] = 2x\). The equilibrium condition is: \[K = \frac{[B]^2}{[A]} = \frac{(2x)^2}{a_0 - x} = \frac{4x^2}{a_0 - x}\]

Rearranging: \(K(a_0 - x) = 4x^2\), or \(4x^2 + Kx - Ka_0 = 0\).

function chemical_equilibrium(K, a0)
    # Solve: 4x² + Kx - Ka₀ = 0
    # Equivalent to finding root of f(x) = 4x² + Kx - Ka₀
    
    f(x) = 4x^2 + K*x - K*a0
    df(x) = 8x + K
    
    # Physical constraint: 0 < x < a₀
    x_guess = a0 / 2
    
    x_eq = newton(f, df, x_guess)
    
    # Calculate concentrations
    A_eq = a0 - x_eq
    B_eq = 2 * x_eq
    
    println("Equilibrium extent: x = $x_eq")
    println("[A]_eq = $A_eq")
    println("[B]_eq = $B_eq")
    println("Check K = [B]²/[A] = $(B_eq^2 / A_eq)")
    
    return x_eq, A_eq, B_eq
end

# Example: K = 0.1, initial [A] = 1.0 M
chemical_equilibrium(0.1, 1.0)

2.10.3 Example 2.3: Van der Waals Equation of State

The van der Waals equation relates pressure, volume, and temperature for real gases: \[\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT\]

Given \(P\), \(T\), and van der Waals constants \(a\) and \(b\), find the molar volume \(V\).

function van_der_waals_volume(P, T, a, b, R=8.314)
    # Rearrange to standard form: f(V) = 0
    # PV³ - (Pb + RT)V² + aV - ab = 0
    
    f(V) = P*V^3 - (P*b + R*T)*V^2 + a*V - a*b
    df(V) = 3*P*V^2 - 2*(P*b + R*T)*V + a
    
    # Initial guess: ideal gas law V₀ = RT/P
    V0 = R * T / P
    
    try
        V = newton(f, df, V0)
        println("Molar volume: V = $V m³/mol")
        
        # Verify the equation
        lhs = (P + a/V^2) * (V - b)
        rhs = R * T
        println("Verification: (P + a/V²)(V - b) = $lhs")
        println("RT = $rhs")
        println("Relative error: $(abs(lhs - rhs)/rhs)")
        
        return V
    catch e
        println("Newton's method failed: $e")
        
        # Try with multiple initial guesses
        for V_init in [0.1*V0, 0.5*V0, V0, 2*V0, 5*V0]
            try
                V = newton(f, df, V_init)
                if V > b  #


\newpage

# Chapter 3: Polynomial Interpolation and Approximation

The problem of approximating a function by a polynomial lies at the heart of numerical analysis. Given data points $(x_0, y_0), (x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ where $y_i = f(x_i)$ for some function $f$, we seek a polynomial $p(x)$ that passes through all these points. This interpolation problem has a unique solution in the space of polynomials of degree at most $n$, but the quality of this approximation depends critically on the choice of interpolation points and the degree of the polynomial.

This chapter develops the theory and practice of polynomial interpolation and approximation. We begin with the classical Lagrange and Newton forms of the interpolating polynomial, then examine the Runge phenomenon that reveals the dangers of high-degree polynomial interpolation. We introduce Chebyshev nodes as a near-optimal choice of interpolation points, followed by piecewise polynomial methods including linear interpolation and cubic splines. Finally, we explore approximation theory, including the Weierstrass approximation theorem and connections to finite element methods.

## 3.1 The Interpolation Problem

> **Definition 3.1 (Polynomial Interpolation).** Given $n+1$ distinct points $x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n$ and corresponding function values $y_0, y_1, \ldots, y_n$, the polynomial interpolation problem seeks a polynomial $p(x)$ of degree at most $n$ such that $p(x_i) = y_i$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$.

> **Theorem 3.1 (Existence and Uniqueness).** The polynomial interpolation problem has a unique solution in $\mathbb{P}_n$, the space of polynomials of degree at most $n$.

*Proof.* We seek coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$ such that $p(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n a_k x^k$ satisfies the interpolation conditions. This gives us the linear system:
$$\begin{bmatrix}
1 & x_0 & x_0^2 & \cdots & x_0^n \\
1 & x_1 & x_1^2 & \cdots & x_1^n \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & x_n & x_n^2 & \cdots & x_n^n
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_0 \\ a_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_n
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
y_0 \\ y_1 \\ \vdots \\ y_n
\end{bmatrix}$$

The coefficient matrix is the Vandermonde matrix with determinant $\prod_{0 \leq i < j \leq n} (x_j - x_i) \neq 0$ since the points are distinct. Therefore, the system has a unique solution. □

## 3.2 Lagrange Interpolation

Rather than solving the Vandermonde system directly, we construct the interpolating polynomial using Lagrange basis functions.

> **Definition 3.2 (Lagrange Basis Functions).** For interpolation points $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n$, the $k$-th Lagrange basis function is:
$$L_k(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq k}}^n \frac{x - x_j}{x_k - x_j}$$

These functions satisfy the crucial property $L_k(x_i) = \delta_{ki}$ where $\delta_{ki}$ is the Kronecker delta.

> **Theorem 3.2 (Lagrange Interpolation Formula).** The unique interpolating polynomial is:
$$p(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n y_k L_k(x)$$

*Proof.* By construction, $p(x_i) = \sum_{k=0}^n y_k L_k(x_i) = \sum_{k=0}^n y_k \delta_{ki} = y_i$. Since $p(x) \in \mathbb{P}_n$ and satisfies the interpolation conditions, uniqueness implies this is the interpolating polynomial. □

### Example 3.1: Lagrange Interpolation

Consider interpolating $f(x) = \sin(x)$ at the points $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = \pi/2$, $x_2 = \pi$ with values $y_0 = 0$, $y_1 = 1$, $y_2 = 0$.

The Lagrange basis functions are:
- $L_0(x) = \frac{(x - \pi/2)(x - \pi)}{(0 - \pi/2)(0 - \pi)} = \frac{2(x - \pi/2)(x - \pi)}{\pi^2}$
- $L_1(x) = \frac{x(x - \pi)}{(\pi/2)((\pi/2) - \pi)} = \frac{-4x(x - \pi)}{\pi^2}$
- $L_2(x) = \frac{x(x - \pi/2)}{\pi(\pi - \pi/2)} = \frac{2x(x - \pi/2)}{\pi^2}$

The interpolating polynomial is:
$$p(x) = 0 \cdot L_0(x) + 1 \cdot L_1(x) + 0 \cdot L_2(x) = \frac{-4x(x - \pi)}{\pi^2}$$

```julia
using Plots, LinearAlgebra

# Lagrange interpolation implementation
function lagrange_basis(x, nodes, k)
    L = 1.0
    for j in eachindex(nodes)
        if j != k
            L *= (x - nodes[j]) / (nodes[k] - nodes[j])
        end
    end
    return L
end

function lagrange_interpolate(x, nodes, values)
    n = length(nodes)
    p = 0.0
    for k in 1:n
        p += values[k] * lagrange_basis(x, nodes, k)
    end
    return p
end

# Example: interpolating sin(x)
nodes = [0.0, π/2, π]
values = [sin(x) for x in nodes]

x_plot = range(0, π, length=1000)
p_vals = [lagrange_interpolate(x, nodes, values) for x in x_plot]
f_vals = sin.(x_plot)

plot(x_plot, f_vals, label="sin(x)", lw=2)
plot!(x_plot, p_vals, label="Lagrange interpolant", lw=2)
scatter!(nodes, values, label="Interpolation points", ms=6)
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("y")
title!("Lagrange Interpolation of sin(x)")

2.11 3.3 Newton Interpolation and Divided Differences

The Lagrange form, while conceptually clear, is computationally inefficient when adding new interpolation points. Newton’s form provides a more practical approach using divided differences.

Definition 3.3 (Divided Differences). The divided differences are defined recursively: - Zeroth-order: \(f[x_i] = f(x_i) = y_i\) - First-order: \(f[x_i, x_j] = \frac{f[x_j] - f[x_i]}{x_j - x_i}\) - Higher-order: \(f[x_{i_0}, x_{i_1}, \ldots, x_{i_k}] = \frac{f[x_{i_1}, \ldots, x_{i_k}] - f[x_{i_0}, \ldots, x_{i_{k-1}}]}{x_{i_k} - x_{i_0}}\)

Theorem 3.3 (Newton Interpolation Formula). The interpolating polynomial can be written as: \[p(x) = f[x_0] + f[x_0, x_1](x - x_0) + f[x_0, x_1, x_2](x - x_0)(x - x_1) + \cdots + f[x_0, \ldots, x_n]\prod_{j=0}^{n-1}(x - x_j)\]

The Newton form has the advantage that adding a new interpolation point requires computing only one new divided difference and one additional term.

# Newton interpolation with divided differences
function divided_differences(nodes, values)
    n = length(nodes)
    # Create divided difference table
    dd_table = zeros(n, n)
    dd_table[:, 1] = values
    
    for j in 2:n
        for i in 1:(n-j+1)
            dd_table[i, j] = (dd_table[i+1, j-1] - dd_table[i, j-1]) / 
                           (nodes[i+j-1] - nodes[i])
        end
    end
    
    return dd_table[1, :]  # First row contains the coefficients
end

function newton_interpolate(x, nodes, coeffs)
    n = length(nodes)
    p = coeffs[1]
    term = 1.0
    
    for i in 2:n
        term *= (x - nodes[i-1])
        p += coeffs[i] * term
    end
    
    return p
end

# Example: Newton form for the same sin(x) interpolation
nodes = [0.0, π/2, π]
values = [sin(x) for x in nodes]
coeffs = divided_differences(nodes, values)

println("Newton coefficients: ", coeffs)

# Verify equivalence with Lagrange form
x_test = π/4
lag_val = lagrange_interpolate(x_test, nodes, values)
new_val = newton_interpolate(x_test, nodes, coeffs)
println("Lagrange at π/4: ", lag_val)
println("Newton at π/4: ", new_val)
println("Difference: ", abs(lag_val - new_val))

2.12 3.4 The Runge Phenomenon

A natural question arises: as we increase the number of interpolation points, does the polynomial interpolant converge to the function being interpolated? Unfortunately, the answer is generally no when using equally spaced points.

Definition 3.4 (Runge Phenomenon). The Runge phenomenon refers to the oscillatory behavior and divergence that occurs when interpolating a smooth function with high-degree polynomials using equally spaced points, particularly near the boundaries of the interpolation interval.

2.12.1 Example 3.2: Runge Function

Consider Runge’s function \(f(x) = \frac{1}{1 + 25x^2}\) on the interval \([-1, 1]\). This function is analytic everywhere, yet polynomial interpolation with equally spaced points fails catastrophically.

# Demonstrate the Runge phenomenon
runge(x) = 1 / (1 + 25 * x^2)

function demonstrate_runge_phenomenon()
    x_fine = range(-1, 1, length=1000)
    f_exact = runge.(x_fine)
    
    p = plot(x_fine, f_exact, label="Runge function", lw=3, color=:black)
    
    degrees = [5, 10, 15, 20]
    colors = [:blue, :red, :green, :orange]
    
    for (i, n) in enumerate(degrees)
        # Equally spaced interpolation points
        nodes = range(-1, 1, length=n+1)
        values = runge.(nodes)
        coeffs = divided_differences(collect(nodes), collect(values))
        
        p_vals = [newton_interpolate(x, collect(nodes), coeffs) for x in x_fine]
        
        plot!(p, x_fine, p_vals, label="Degree $n", lw=2, color=colors[i])
        scatter!(p, nodes, values, color=colors[i], ms=4, alpha=0.7, label="")
    end
    
    ylims!(p, -2, 2)
    xlabel!(p, "x")
    ylabel!(p, "f(x)")
    title!(p, "Runge Phenomenon with Equally Spaced Points")
    
    return p
end

demonstrate_runge_phenomenon()

The oscillations grow without bound as the degree increases, demonstrating that high-degree polynomial interpolation with equally spaced points is fundamentally unstable.

2.13 3.5 Chebyshev Nodes and Near-Optimal Interpolation

The Runge phenomenon motivates the search for better choices of interpolation points. The key insight is that the interpolation error depends on the product \(\prod_{j=0}^n (x - x_j)\), which we want to minimize.

Definition 3.5 (Chebyshev Polynomials). The Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind are defined by: \[T_n(\cos \theta) = \cos(n\theta)\] or equivalently by the recurrence: - \(T_0(x) = 1\) - \(T_1(x) = x\)
- \(T_{n+1}(x) = 2xT_n(x) - T_{n-1}(x)\)

Definition 3.6 (Chebyshev Nodes). The Chebyshev nodes of the first kind on \([-1, 1]\) are: \[x_k = \cos\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2(n+1)}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n\]

These are the roots of \(T_{n+1}(x)\).

Theorem 3.4 (Optimal Interpolation Points). Among all possible choices of \(n+1\) distinct interpolation points in \([-1, 1]\), the Chebyshev nodes minimize the maximum value of \(|\prod_{j=0}^n (x - x_j)|\) over \(x \in [-1, 1]\).

The interpolation error theorem provides the theoretical foundation:

Theorem 3.5 (Interpolation Error Bound). If \(f \in C^{n+1}[a,b]\) and \(p\) is the polynomial interpolating \(f\) at points \(x_0, \ldots, x_n\), then for any \(x \in [a,b]\): \[|f(x) - p(x)| \leq \frac{|f^{(n+1)}(\xi)|}{(n+1)!} \left|\prod_{j=0}^n (x - x_j)\right|\] for some \(\xi \in [a,b]\).

2.13.1 Example 3.3: Chebyshev Interpolation of Runge Function

function chebyshev_nodes(n, a=-1, b=1)
    # Chebyshev nodes on [a,b]
    nodes = [cos((2*k + 1) * π / (2*(n+1))) for k in 0:n]
    # Map from [-1,1] to [a,b]
    return [(b + a)/2 .+ (b - a)/2 * nodes]
end

function compare_interpolation_strategies()
    n = 15  # Degree of interpolation
    x_fine = range(-1, 1, length=1000)
    f_exact = runge.(x_fine)
    
    # Equally spaced points
    nodes_equal = range(-1, 1, length=n+1)
    values_equal = runge.(nodes_equal)
    coeffs_equal = divided_differences(collect(nodes_equal), collect(values_equal))
    p_equal = [newton_interpolate(x, collect(nodes_equal), coeffs_equal) for x in x_fine]
    
    # Chebyshev points
    nodes_cheb = chebyshev_nodes(n)
    values_cheb = runge.(nodes_cheb)
    coeffs_cheb = divided_differences(nodes_cheb, values_cheb)
    p_cheb = [newton_interpolate(x, nodes_cheb, coeffs_cheb) for x in x_fine]
    
    p1 = plot(x_fine, f_exact, label="Exact", lw=3, color=:black)
    plot!(p1, x_fine, p_equal, label="Equal spacing", lw=2, color=:red)
    scatter!(p1, nodes_equal, values_equal, color=:red, ms=3, alpha=0.7, label="")
    ylims!(p1, -0.5, 1.5)
    title!(p1, "Equally Spaced Points (Degree 15)")
    
    p2 = plot(x_fine, f_exact, label="Exact", lw=3, color=:black)
    plot!(p2, x_fine, p_cheb, label="Chebyshev nodes", lw=2, color=:blue)
    scatter!(p2, nodes_cheb, values_cheb, color=:blue, ms=3, alpha=0.7, label="")
    ylims!(p2, -0.5, 1.5)
    title!(p2, "Chebyshev Nodes (Degree 15)")
    
    plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1), size=(800, 600))
end

compare_interpolation_strategies()

# Compute maximum interpolation errors
function interpolation_error_comparison()
    degrees = 5:2:25
    max_errors_equal = Float64[]
    max_errors_cheb = Float64[]
    
    x_test = range(-0.95, 0.95, length=500)  # Avoid endpoints for stability
    f_test = runge.(x_test)
    
    for n in degrees
        # Equally spaced
        nodes_equal = range(-1, 1, length=n+1)
        values_equal = runge.(nodes_equal)
        coeffs_equal = divided_differences(collect(nodes_equal), collect(values_equal))
        p_equal = [newton_interpolate(x, collect(nodes_equal), coeffs_equal) for x in x_test]
        
        # Chebyshev
        nodes_cheb = chebyshev_nodes(n)
        values_cheb = runge.(nodes_cheb)
        coeffs_cheb = divided_differences(nodes_cheb, values_cheb)
        p_cheb = [newton_interpolate(x, nodes_cheb, coeffs_cheb) for x in x_test]
        
        push!(max_errors_equal, maximum(abs.(f_test - p_equal)))
        push!(max_errors_cheb, maximum(abs.(f_test - p_cheb)))
    end
    
    plot(degrees, max_errors_equal, label="Equal spacing", 
         yscale=:log10, marker=:circle, lw=2)
    plot!(degrees, max_errors_cheb, label="Chebyshev nodes", 
          yscale=:log10, marker=:square, lw=2)
    xlabel!("Polynomial degree")
    ylabel!("Maximum interpolation error")
    title!("Interpolation Error vs. Polynomial Degree")
end

interpolation_error_comparison()

2.14 3.6 Piecewise Polynomial Interpolation

High-degree polynomial interpolation, even with optimal node placement, suffers from practical limitations. An alternative approach uses piecewise polynomials: low-degree polynomials on each subinterval that connect smoothly.

2.14.1 3.6.1 Piecewise Linear Interpolation

The simplest piecewise approach connects adjacent data points with straight lines.

Definition 3.7 (Piecewise Linear Interpolation). Given data points \((x_0, y_0), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\) with \(x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n\), the piecewise linear interpolant is: \[p(x) = y_i + \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{x_{i+1} - x_i}(x - x_i), \quad x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}]\]

function piecewise_linear(x, nodes, values)
    n = length(nodes)
    if x <= nodes[1]
        return values[1]
    elseif x >= nodes[end]
        return values[end]
    end
    
    # Find the interval containing x
    i = searchsortedlast(nodes, x)
    if i == n
        return values[n]
    end
    
    # Linear interpolation in [nodes[i], nodes[i+1]]
    t = (x - nodes[i]) / (nodes[i+1] - nodes[i])
    return (1 - t) * values[i] + t * values[i+1]
end

# Example: piecewise linear vs high-degree polynomial
function compare_piecewise_vs_polynomial()
    # Dense sampling of Runge function
    nodes_dense = range(-1, 1, length=11)
    values_dense = runge.(nodes_dense)
    
    x_plot = range(-1, 1, length=1000)
    f_exact = runge.(x_plot)
    
    # High-degree polynomial interpolation (Chebyshev nodes)
    nodes_cheb = chebyshev_nodes(10)
    values_cheb = runge.(nodes_cheb)
    coeffs_cheb = divided_differences(nodes_cheb, values_cheb)
    p_poly = [newton_interpolate(x, nodes_cheb, coeffs_cheb) for x in x_plot]
    
    # Piecewise linear interpolation
    p_linear = [piecewise_linear(x, collect(nodes_dense), collect(values_dense)) 
                for x in x_plot]
    
    plot(x_plot, f_exact, label="Exact", lw=3, color=:black)
    plot!(x_plot, p_poly, label="Polynomial (deg 10)", lw=2, color=:blue)
    plot!(x_plot, p_linear, label="Piecewise linear", lw=2, color=:red)
    scatter!(nodes_dense, values_dense, color=:red, ms=4, alpha=0.7, label="")
    xlabel!("x")
    ylabel!("f(x)")
    title!("Piecewise Linear vs Polynomial Interpolation")
end

compare_piecewise_vs_polynomial()

2.14.2 3.6.2 Cubic Spline Interpolation

While piecewise linear interpolation avoids oscillations, it produces non-smooth approximations. Cubic splines provide \(C^2\) continuity while maintaining stability.

Definition 3.8 (Cubic Spline). A cubic spline interpolating data \((x_i, y_i)\) for \(i = 0, 1, \ldots, n\) is a function \(S(x)\) such that: 1. \(S(x)\) is a cubic polynomial on each interval \([x_i, x_{i+1}]\) 2. \(S(x_i) = y_i\) for all \(i\) 3. \(S(x)\), \(S'(x)\), and \(S''(x)\) are continuous

On each interval \([x_i, x_{i+1}]\), we can write: \[S(x) = a_i + b_i(x - x_i) + c_i(x - x_i)^2 + d_i(x - x_i)^3\]

The continuity conditions at interior points give us a system of equations for the coefficients.

Definition 3.9 (Natural Cubic Spline). A natural cubic spline satisfies the additional boundary conditions \(S''(x_0) = S''(x_n) = 0\).

using SparseArrays

function natural_cubic_spline(nodes, values)
    n = length(nodes) - 1  # number of intervals
    h = diff(nodes)        # interval lengths
    
    # Set up the tridiagonal system for second derivatives
    # Based on the condition that S'(x_i-) = S'(x_i+)
    A = spzeros(n+1, n+1)
    b = zeros(n+1)
    
    # Natural boundary conditions: S''(x_0) = S''(x_n) = 0
    A[1, 1] = 1.0
    A[n+1, n+1] = 1.0
    b[1] = 0.0
    b[n+1] = 0.0
    
    # Interior equations from continuity of first derivative
    for i in 2:n
        A[i, i-1] = h[i-1]
        A[i, i]   = 2 * (h[i-1] + h[i])
        A[i, i+1] = h[i]
        b[i] = 6 * ((values[i+1] - values[i])/h[i] - 
                   (values[i] - values[i-1])/h[i-1])
    end
    
    # Solve for second derivatives
    c = A \ b
    
    # Compute spline coefficients for each interval
    coefficients = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n, 4)  # [a, b, c, d] for each interval
    
    for i in 1:n
        coefficients[i, 1] = values[i]  # a_i
        coefficients[i, 2] = (values[i+1] - values[i])/h[i] - 
                           h[i] * (2*c[i] + c[i+1])/6  # b_i
        coefficients[i, 3] = c[i]/2  # c_i
        coefficients[i, 4] = (c[i+1] - c[i])/(6*h[i])  # d_i
    end
    
    return coefficients
end

function evaluate_spline(x, nodes, coefficients)
    n = length(nodes) - 1
    
    # Find the interval containing x
    if x <= nodes[1]
        i = 1
    elseif x >= nodes[end]
        i = n
    else
        i = searchsortedlast(nodes, x)
        if i == 0; i = 1; end
        if i > n; i = n; end
    end
    
    # Evaluate the spline in interval i
    dx = x - nodes[i]
    a, b, c, d = coefficients[i, :]
    return a + b*dx + c*dx^2 + d*dx^3
end

# Example: cubic spline interpolation
function demonstrate_cubic_splines()
    # Use fewer points to show the smoothing effect
    nodes = [-1.0, -0.5, 0.0, 0.5, 1.0]
    values = runge.(nodes)
    
    # Compute spline coefficients
    spline_coeffs = natural_cubic_spline(nodes, values)
    
    x_plot = range(-1, 1, length=1000)
    f_exact = runge.(x_plot)
    
    # Evaluate spline
    s_vals = [evaluate_spline(x, nodes, spline_coeffs) for x in x_plot]
    
    # Compare with piecewise linear
    p_linear = [piecewise_linear(x, nodes, values) for x in x_plot]
    
    plot(x_plot, f_exact, label="Exact", lw=3, color=:black)
    plot!(x_plot, s_vals, label="Cubic spline", lw=2, color=:blue)
    plot!(x_plot, p_linear, label="Piecewise linear", lw=2, color=:red, alpha=0.7)
    scatter!(nodes, values, color=:black, ms=6, label="Data points")
    xlabel!("x")
    ylabel!("f(x)")
    title!("Cubic Spline vs Piecewise Linear Interpolation")
end

demonstrate_cubic_splines()

2.14.3 3.6.3 Clamped and Not-a-Knot Splines

Natural splines impose zero second derivative at the boundaries, which may not reflect the true behavior of the function. Alternative boundary conditions include:

Definition 3.10 (Clamped Cubic Spline). A clamped cubic spline specifies the first derivatives at the endpoints: \(S'(x_0) = f'(x_0)\) and \(S'(x_n) = f'(x_n)\).

Definition 3.11 (Not-a-Knot Spline). A not-a-knot spline requires that \(S'''(x)\) be continuous at \(x_1\) and \(x_{n-1}\), effectively making the spline a single cubic polynomial on \([x_0, x_2]\) and \([x_{n-2}, x_n]\).

function clamped_cubic_spline(nodes, values, left_deriv, right_deriv)
    n = length(nodes) - 1
    h = diff(nodes)
    
    A = spzeros(n+1, n+1)
    b = zeros(n+1)
    
    # Clamped boundary conditions
    A[1, 1] = 2*h[1]
    A[1, 2] = h[1]
    b[1] = 6*((values[2] - values[1])/h[1] - left_deriv)
    
    A[n+1, n] = h[n]
    A[n+1, n+1] = 2*h[n]
    b[n+1] = 6*(right_deriv - (values[n+1] - values[n])/h[n])
    
    # Interior equations
    for i in 2:n
        A[i, i-1] = h[i-1]
        A[i, i]   = 2 * (h[i-1] + h[i])
        A[i, i+1] = h[i]
        b[i] = 6 * ((values[i+1] - values[i])/h[i] - 
                   (values[i] - values[i-1])/h[i-1])
    end
    
    c = A \ b
    
    # Compute coefficients as before
    coefficients = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n, 4)
    for i in 1:n
        coefficients[i, 1] = values[i]
        coefficients[i, 2] = (values[i+1] - values[i])/h[i] - 
                           h[i] * (2*c[i] + c[i+1])/6
        coefficients[i, 3] = c[i]/2
        coefficients[i, 4] = (c[i+1] - c[i])/(6*h[i])
    end
    
    return coefficients
end

# Example comparing different spline boundary conditions
function compare_spline_boundary_conditions()
    runge_deriv(x) = -50*x / (1 + 25*x^2)^2  # derivative of Runge function
    
    nodes = range(-1, 1, length=6)
    values = runge.(nodes)
    
    # Natural spline
    natural_coeffs = natural_cubic_spline(collect(nodes), collect(values))
    
    # Clamped spline with exact derivatives
    left_deriv = runge_deriv(nodes[1])
    right_deriv = runge_deriv(nodes[end])
    clamped_coeffs = clamped_cubic_spline(collect(nodes), collect(values), 
                                        left_deriv, right_deriv)
    
    x_plot = range(-1, 1, length=1000)
    f_exact = runge.(x_plot)
    
    natural_vals = [evaluate_spline(x, collect(nodes), natural_coeffs)


\newpage

# Chapter 4: Numerical Differentiation and Integration

The fundamental operations of calculus—differentiation and integration—rarely admit closed-form solutions for functions encountered in scientific computing. This chapter develops the computational analogs: finite difference methods for approximating derivatives and quadrature rules for approximating integrals. These methods underpin virtually every numerical algorithm in this textbook, from solving differential equations to optimizing complex functions.

## 4.1 Finite Difference Methods

### Forward, Backward, and Central Differences

The derivative of a function f at point x is defined as the limit
f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h

Since we cannot take this limit computationally, we approximate it using finite differences with small but non-zero h.

> **Definition 4.1 (Finite Difference Formulas).** Let f be a function and h > 0 be a step size. The finite difference approximations to f'(x) are:
> - **Forward difference**: f'(x) ≈ [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h
> - **Backward difference**: f'(x) ≈ [f(x) - f(x-h)]/h  
> - **Central difference**: f'(x) ≈ [f(x+h) - f(x-h)]/(2h)

The accuracy of these approximations depends critically on the step size h and the smoothness of f. Too large an h introduces discretization error; too small an h amplifies roundoff error from floating-point arithmetic.

### Truncation Error Analysis

To analyze the accuracy of finite differences, we employ Taylor series expansions around the point x.

> **Theorem 4.2 (Forward Difference Error).** If f ∈ C²[x, x+h], then the forward difference formula satisfies
> f'(x) - [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h = -h/2 · f''(ξ)
> for some ξ ∈ (x, x+h). The truncation error is O(h).

*Proof.* Expanding f(x+h) in a Taylor series about x:
f(x+h) = f(x) + h·f'(x) + (h²/2)·f''(ξ)

Rearranging:
[f(x+h) - f(x)]/h = f'(x) + (h/2)·f''(ξ)

The result follows immediately. □

> **Theorem 4.3 (Central Difference Error).** If f ∈ C⁴[x-h, x+h], then the central difference formula satisfies
> f'(x) - [f(x+h) - f(x-h)]/(2h) = -(h²/6)·f'''(ξ)
> for some ξ ∈ (x-h, x+h). The truncation error is O(h²).

*Proof.* Expanding both f(x+h) and f(x-h):
f(x+h) = f(x) + h·f'(x) + (h²/2)·f''(x) + (h³/6)·f'''(x) + (h⁴/24)·f⁽⁴⁾(ξ₁)
f(x-h) = f(x) - h·f'(x) + (h²/2)·f''(x) - (h³/6)·f'''(x) + (h⁴/24)·f⁽⁴⁾(ξ₂)

Subtracting and dividing by 2h:
[f(x+h) - f(x-h)]/(2h) = f'(x) + (h²/6)·f'''(x) + (h³/24)·[f⁽⁴⁾(ξ₁) + f⁽⁴⁾(ξ₂)]

By the intermediate value theorem, there exists ξ such that f⁽⁴⁾(ξ) = [f⁽⁴⁾(ξ₁) + f⁽⁴⁾(ξ₂)]/2, giving the stated result. □

The central difference method achieves second-order accuracy compared to first-order for forward/backward differences—a significant improvement for smooth functions.

**Example 4.1.** Consider f(x) = e^x at x = 1. We compare finite difference approximations with the exact derivative f'(1) = e ≈ 2.718281828.

```julia
using Printf

function finite_differences_demo()
    f(x) = exp(x)
    exact_derivative = exp(1.0)
    x = 1.0
    
    println("Step size h    Forward diff   Backward diff  Central diff")
    println("              Error          Error          Error")
    
    for i in 1:10
        h = 10.0^(-i)
        
        # Forward difference
        forward = (f(x + h) - f(x)) / h
        forward_error = abs(forward - exact_derivative)
        
        # Backward difference  
        backward = (f(x) - f(x - h)) / h
        backward_error = abs(backward - exact_derivative)
        
        # Central difference
        central = (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2h)
        central_error = abs(central - exact_derivative)
        
        @printf("1e-%02d        %.2e       %.2e       %.2e\n", 
                i, forward_error, backward_error, central_error)
    end
end

finite_differences_demo()

This demonstrates that central differences achieve quadratic convergence until roundoff error dominates for very small h.

2.14.4 Higher-Order Derivatives

Finite differences extend naturally to higher-order derivatives using repeated application or direct construction from Taylor expansions.

Definition 4.4 (Second Derivative Approximation). The central difference approximation to f’‘(x) is: f’’(x) ≈ [f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)]/h²

This formula has truncation error O(h²) and arises from the Taylor expansion: f(x±h) = f(x) ± h·f’(x) + (h²/2)·f’‘(x) ± (h³/6)·f’’’(x) + O(h⁴)

Adding f(x+h) + f(x-h) - 2f(x) eliminates the first-derivative terms.

2.14.5 Richardson Extrapolation

Richardson extrapolation systematically improves the accuracy of finite difference formulas by combining approximations at different step sizes.

Theorem 4.5 (Richardson Extrapolation). Suppose a numerical method produces approximation A(h) with error expansion A(h) = A + c₁h^p + c₂h^{p+1} + … where A is the exact value. Then the Richardson extrapolation A_{improved} = (2^p · A(h/2) - A(h))/(2^p - 1) has error O(h^{p+1}).

For central differences with p = 2: f’(x) ≈ (4·D(h/2) - D(h))/3

where D(h) = [f(x+h) - f(x-h)]/(2h).

Example 4.2. We apply Richardson extrapolation to improve central difference accuracy:

function richardson_extrapolation_demo()
    f(x) = sin(x^2)  # f'(x) = 2x*cos(x^2)
    fprime(x) = 2x * cos(x^2)
    x = 1.5
    exact = fprime(x)
    
    h = 0.1
    D_h = (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2h)
    D_h2 = (f(x + h/2) - f(x - h/2)) / h  # Central diff with h/2
    
    # Richardson extrapolation
    D_richardson = (4 * D_h2 - D_h) / 3
    
    println("Exact derivative: $(exact)")
    println("Central diff (h):   $(D_h), error: $(abs(D_h - exact))")
    println("Central diff (h/2): $(D_h2), error: $(abs(D_h2 - exact))")
    println("Richardson extrap:  $(D_richardson), error: $(abs(D_richardson - exact))")
end

richardson_extrapolation_demo()

Richardson extrapolation typically reduces the error by 1-2 orders of magnitude.

2.15 4.2 Automatic Differentiation

Modern scientific computing—particularly machine learning and scientific machine learning—relies heavily on automatic differentiation (AD) rather than finite differences for computing derivatives. AD provides machine-precision derivatives efficiently.

2.15.1 Forward Mode Automatic Differentiation

Forward mode AD computes derivatives by propagating dual numbers through the computational graph.

Definition 4.6 (Dual Numbers). A dual number is an element of the form a + bε where a, b ∈ ℝ and ε² = 0. The set of dual numbers forms the algebra ℝ[ε]/(ε²).

Arithmetic operations on dual numbers satisfy: - (a₁ + b₁ε) + (a₂ + b₂ε) = (a₁ + a₂) + (b₁ + b₂)ε - (a₁ + b₁ε) × (a₂ + b₂ε) = a₁a₂ + (a₁b₂ + a₂b₁)ε

For elementary functions f(a + bε) = f(a) + bf’(a)ε.

Theorem 4.7 (Forward Mode Chain Rule). If y = f(g(x)) and we evaluate f(g(x + ε)) using dual arithmetic, the result is y + y’ε where y’ = f’(g(x))g’(x).

using ForwardDiff

# Example: Compute derivative of f(x) = x^3 * exp(sin(x)) at x = 2.0
function complex_function(x)
    return x^3 * exp(sin(x))
end

x = 2.0
derivative = ForwardDiff.derivative(complex_function, x)

# Manual verification using finite differences
h = 1e-8
finite_diff = (complex_function(x + h) - complex_function(x - h)) / (2h)

println("Automatic differentiation: $(derivative)")
println("Finite difference:         $(finite_diff)")
println("Absolute difference:       $(abs(derivative - finite_diff))")

2.15.2 Reverse Mode Automatic Differentiation

Reverse mode AD (backpropagation) computes gradients efficiently for functions ℝⁿ → ℝ by traversing the computational graph backwards.

Theorem 4.8 (Reverse Mode Efficiency). For f: ℝⁿ → ℝ, forward mode requires n forward passes to compute ∇f, while reverse mode requires one forward pass and one backward pass regardless of n.

using Zygote

# Example: Gradient of multivariate function
function multivariate_function(x)
    return sum(x.^3) + exp(sum(sin.(x))) + dot(x, x)
end

x = [1.0, 2.0, -0.5, 3.0]

# Automatic differentiation gradient
grad_ad = Zygote.gradient(multivariate_function, x)[1]

# Finite difference verification (very expensive for high dimensions!)
function finite_diff_gradient(f, x; h=1e-6)
    n = length(x)
    grad = zeros(n)
    for i in 1:n
        x_plus = copy(x)
        x_minus = copy(x)
        x_plus[i] += h
        x_minus[i] -= h
        grad[i] = (f(x_plus) - f(x_minus)) / (2h)
    end
    return grad
end

grad_fd = finite_diff_gradient(multivariate_function, x)

println("AD gradient:  $(grad_ad)")
println("FD gradient:  $(grad_fd)")
println("Max difference: $(maximum(abs.(grad_ad .- grad_fd)))")

For machine learning applications with millions of parameters, reverse mode AD makes gradient-based optimization computationally feasible.

2.16 4.3 Numerical Integration

Numerical quadrature approximates definite integrals ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx when analytical integration is impossible or impractical. The fundamental approach replaces the continuous integral with a weighted sum of function evaluations.

2.16.1 Newton-Cotes Formulas

Newton-Cotes methods approximate the integrand with polynomial interpolation over equally spaced points.

Definition 4.9 (Trapezoidal Rule). The trapezoidal rule approximates ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx ≈ (b-a)/2 · [f(a) + f(b)].

Theorem 4.10 (Trapezoidal Rule Error). If f ∈ C²[a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx - (b-a)/2 · [f(a) + f(b)] = -(b-a)³/12 · f’’(ξ) for some ξ ∈ (a,b). The error is O(h³) where h = b-a.

The composite trapezoidal rule divides [a,b] into n subintervals:

Definition 4.11 (Composite Trapezoidal Rule). ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx ≈ h/2 · [f(a) + 2∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ⁻¹ f(a + ih) + f(b)] where h = (b-a)/n.

Definition 4.12 (Simpson’s Rule). Simpson’s rule uses quadratic interpolation: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx ≈ (b-a)/6 · [f(a) + 4f((a+b)/2) + f(b)]

The error for Simpson’s rule is O(h⁵) for smooth functions, making it significantly more accurate than the trapezoidal rule.

Example 4.3. We compare quadrature methods on ∫₀^π sin(x) dx = 2:

function quadrature_comparison()
    f(x) = sin(x)
    a, b = 0.0, π
    exact = 2.0
    
    println("n        Trapezoidal    Simpson       Errors")
    
    for n in [4, 8, 16, 32, 64]
        h = (b - a) / n
        x = range(a, b, length=n+1)
        
        # Composite trapezoidal rule
        trap = h/2 * (f(a) + 2*sum(f.(x[2:end-1])) + f(b))
        trap_error = abs(trap - exact)
        
        # Composite Simpson's rule (requires even n)
        if n % 2 == 0
            simp = h/3 * (f(a) + 4*sum(f.(x[2:2:end-1])) + 
                         2*sum(f.(x[3:2:end-2])) + f(b))
            simp_error = abs(simp - exact)
            @printf("%2d       %.6f      %.6f        %.2e  %.2e\n", 
                    n, trap, simp, trap_error, simp_error)
        else
            @printf("%2d       %.6f      N/A          %.2e  N/A\n", 
                    n, trap, trap_error)
        end
    end
end

quadrature_comparison()

2.16.2 Gaussian Quadrature

Gaussian quadrature achieves higher accuracy by optimizing both the node locations and weights, not just the weights as in Newton-Cotes methods.

Theorem 4.13 (Gaussian Quadrature). The n-point Gaussian quadrature rule ∫₋₁¹ f(x) dx ≈ ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ wᵢ f(xᵢ) where {xᵢ} are the roots of the n-th Legendre polynomial and {wᵢ} are the corresponding weights, is exact for all polynomials of degree ≤ 2n-1.

The classical Gaussian quadrature uses Legendre polynomials on [-1,1]. For general intervals [a,b], we use the transformation x = ((b-a)t + (b+a))/2.

using FastGaussQuadrature

function gaussian_quadrature_demo()
    # Integrate exp(-x^2) from 0 to 2 (no closed form)
    f(x) = exp(-x^2)
    a, b = 0.0, 2.0
    
    println("n-point Gauss   Approximation")
    
    for n in [5, 10, 15, 20, 25]
        # Get Gauss-Legendre nodes and weights for [-1,1]
        nodes, weights = gausslegendre(n)
        
        # Transform to [a,b]
        x_transformed = (b - a)/2 * nodes .+ (a + b)/2
        w_transformed = (b - a)/2 * weights
        
        # Compute integral
        integral = sum(w_transformed .* f.(x_transformed))
        
        @printf("%2d              %.10f\n", n, integral)
    end
    
    # Compare with adaptive quadrature from QuadGK
    using QuadGK
    adaptive_result, _ = quadgk(f, a, b, rtol=1e-12)
    println("Adaptive quad:  $(adaptive_result)")
end

gaussian_quadrature_demo()

2.16.3 Adaptive Quadrature

Adaptive quadrature automatically adjusts the spacing and number of evaluation points to achieve a specified accuracy tolerance.

Definition 4.14 (Adaptive Strategy). An adaptive quadrature algorithm recursively subdivides intervals where the estimated error exceeds tolerance, concentrating computational effort where the integrand is most difficult to approximate.

The standard approach estimates the error by comparing two quadrature rules of different orders on the same interval.

using QuadGK

function adaptive_quadrature_demo()
    # Integrate a function with sharp features
    f(x) = exp(-100*(x - 0.5)^2) + 0.1*sin(50*x)
    a, b = 0.0, 1.0
    
    # Fixed-grid trapezoidal rule (will miss sharp peak)
    n = 100
    h = (b - a) / n
    x = range(a, b, length=n+1)
    trap_fixed = h/2 * (f(a) + 2*sum(f.(x[2:end-1])) + f(b))
    
    # Adaptive quadrature
    adaptive_result, estimated_error = quadgk(f, a, b, rtol=1e-8)
    
    println("Fixed trapezoidal (n=100): $(trap_fixed)")
    println("Adaptive quadrature:       $(adaptive_result)")
    println("Estimated error:           $(estimated_error)")
    
    # Show how adaptive method refines automatically
    result_tol6, _ = quadgk(f, a, b, rtol=1e-6)
    result_tol10, _ = quadgk(f, a, b, rtol=1e-10)
    result_tol12, _ = quadgk(f, a, b, rtol=1e-12)
    
    println("\nTolerance refinement:")
    println("1e-6:  $(result_tol6)")
    println("1e-10: $(result_tol10)")  
    println("1e-12: $(result_tol12)")
end

adaptive_quadrature_demo()

2.16.4 Monte Carlo Integration

For high-dimensional integrals, Monte Carlo methods often outperform deterministic quadrature, whose computational cost grows exponentially with dimension.

Theorem 4.15 (Monte Carlo Integration). For ∫_D f(x) dx where D ⊂ ℝᵈ has volume V, the Monte Carlo estimator I_N = (V/N) ∑ᵢ₌₁ᴺ f(xᵢ) where {xᵢ} are uniformly distributed random points in D, converges to the exact integral with error O(N^{-1/2}) regardless of dimension d.

Example 4.4. We estimate the volume of a hypersphere using Monte Carlo:

using Random
Random.seed!(42)

function monte_carlo_hypersphere(d, N)
    # Estimate volume of unit hypersphere in d dimensions
    # Embedded in [-1,1]^d hypercube with volume 2^d
    
    count_inside = 0
    for i in 1:N
        x = 2 * rand(d) .- 1  # Random point in [-1,1]^d
        if sum(x.^2) <= 1     # Inside unit sphere
            count_inside += 1
        end
    end
    
    # Volume estimate
    hypercube_volume = 2^d
    sphere_volume_estimate = hypercube_volume * count_inside / N
    
    return sphere_volume_estimate
end

function hypersphere_demo()
    println("Dimension  Samples     MC Estimate   Theoretical")
    
    # Theoretical volumes: V_d = π^{d/2} / Γ(d/2 + 1)
    theoretical_volumes = [2.0, π, 4π/3, π^2/2, 8π^2/15]
    
    for (d, V_exact) in enumerate(theoretical_volumes)
        N = 10^6
        V_mc = monte_carlo_hypersphere(d, N)
        error = abs(V_mc - V_exact)
        
        @printf("%d         %d      %.4f        %.4f     (error: %.3f)\n", 
                d, N, V_mc, V_exact, error)
    end
end

hypersphere_demo()

The key advantage of Monte Carlo: the convergence rate O(N^{-1/2}) is independent of dimension, while deterministic methods suffer from the curse of dimensionality.

2.16.5 Sparse Grids

Sparse grids provide a compromise between full tensor-product grids and Monte Carlo for moderate-dimensional integration.

Definition 4.16 (Sparse Grid Construction). A sparse grid in d dimensions with level L combines tensor products of 1D quadrature rules such that the sum of levels across dimensions is bounded by L, achieving polynomial rather than exponential growth in grid points.
