# Demonstration of sparse grid concept (simplified 2D case)
function sparse_grid_2d_demo()
    # Compare full tensor grid vs sparse grid for 2D integration
    # ∫∫_{[0,1]²} exp(-(x₁² + x₂²)) dx₁ dx₂
    
    f(x1, x2) = exp(-(x1^2 + x2^2))
    
    # Full tensor product grid
    n = 10
    x = range(0, 1, length=n)
    full_grid_points = [(x1, x2) for x1 in x, x2 in x]
    full_grid_sum = sum(f(pt[1], pt[2]) for pt in full_grid_points)
    full_grid_integral = full_grid_sum / n^2  # Assuming uniform weights
    
    println("Full tensor grid ($(n^2) points): $(full_grid_integral)")
    
    # Sparse grid (Smolyak construction, simplified)
    # Uses fewer points by combining 1D rules intelligently
    sparse_points = [(0.5, 0.5)]  # Just the center for illustration
    push!(sparse_points, (0.25, 0.5), (0.75, 0.5))  # Level 1 in x1
    push!(sparse_points, (0.5, 0.25), (0.5, 0.75))  # Level 1 in x2
    push!(sparse_points, (0.25, 0.25), (0.25, 0.75), (0.75, 0.25), (0.75, 0.75))
    
    sparse_sum = sum(f(pt[1], pt[2]) for pt in sparse_points)
    sparse_integral = sparse_sum / length(sparse_points)
    
    println("Sparse grid ($(length(sparse_points)) points): $(sparse_integral)")
    
    # Compare with high-accuracy adaptive quadrature
    using HCubature
    adaptive_result, _ = hcubature(x -> f(x[1], x[2]), [0.0, 0.0], [1.0, 1.0])
    println("Adaptive reference: $(adaptive_result)")
end

sparse_grid_2d_demo()

Real sparse grid implementations (like those in SparseGrids.jl) use sophisticated level-balancing and achieve near-optimal approximation rates for smooth functions.

2.17 4.4 Connections and Applications

The numerical differentiation and integration methods developed in this chapter form the computational backbone for virtually every algorithm in scientific computing:

  • Chapter 8 uses finite differences and automatic differentiation extensively for discretizing ordinary differential equations and computing Jacobians for implicit methods.

  • Optimization methods (covered in advanced courses) rely on automatic differentiation for computing gradients and Hessians efficiently.

  • Finite element and finite difference methods for partial differential equations use numerical integration for assembling system matrices and load vectors.

  • Machine learning algorithms depend critically on reverse-mode automatic differentiation (backpropagation) for training neural networks.

The error analysis techniques introduced here—Taylor series expansions, Richardson extrapolation—extend to more sophisticated numerical methods throughout scientific computing.

2.18 Exercises

Exercise 4.1 (Computational). Implement a general Richardson extrapolation function in Julia that takes a numerical method method(h) and extrapolates to improve accuracy. Test it on central differences for f(x) = x³e^x at x = 1.

Exercise 4.2 (Theoretical). Derive the five-point central difference formula for f’(x) using points x-2h, x-h, x, x+h, x+2h. What is the truncation error order?

Exercise 4.3 (Computational). Compare the computational cost and accuracy of forward-mode automatic differentiation vs. finite differences for computing the gradient of f(x₁,…,x_n) = ∑ᵢ xᵢ^i e^{sin(xᵢ)} for n = 10, 100, 1000.

Exercise 4.4 (Theoretical). Prove that the composite trapezoidal rule has error O(h²) when applied to periodic functions on their period, even though the local truncation error is O(h³).

Exercise 4.5 (Computational). Implement adaptive Simpson’s rule with error estimation. The algorithm should recursively subdivide intervals where |S(a,b) - S(a,m) - S(m,b)| > 15ε where S denotes Simpson’s rule and m = (a+b)/2.

Exercise 4.6 (Computational). Write a Monte Carlo integrator for the d-dimensional Gaussian integral ∫_{ℝᵈ} exp(-||x||²/2) dx. Use importance sampling with a Gaussian proposal distribution. Compare convergence rates for d = 2, 5, 10.

Exercise 4.7 (Theoretical). Show that n-point Gaussian quadrature is exact for polynomials of degree at most 2n-1 by proving that the weights and nodes satisfy a system of 2n nonlinear equations corresponding to polynomial exactness conditions.

Exercise 4.8 (Computational). Implement Romberg integration, which applies Richardson extrapolation to the trapezoidal rule. Create a triangular table where T(k,0) is the 2^k-panel trapezoidal rule and T(k,j) applies j levels of extrapolation.

Exercise 4.9 (Computational). Use automatic differentiation to implement Newton’s method for solving nonlinear systems F(x) = 0 where F: ℝⁿ → ℝⁿ. Test on the system x₁² + x₂² = 1, x₁ - x₂ = 0.5.

Exercise 4.10 (Theoretical). Analyze the stability of the central difference formula f’(x) ≈ [f(x+h) - f(x-h)]/(2h) when function evaluations have relative error ε. Show that the total error is minimized when h = O(ε^{1/3}).

2.19 References

Burden, R. L., & Faires, J. D. (2010). Numerical Analysis (9th ed.). Brooks/Cole.

Davis, P. J., & Rabinowitz, P. (1984). Methods of Numerical Integration (2nd ed.). Academic Press.

Golub, G. H., & Welsch, J. H. (1969). Calculation of Gauss quadrature rules. Mathematics of Computation, 23(106), 221-230.

Griewank, A., & Walther, A. (2008). Evaluating Derivatives: Principles and Techniques of Algorithmic Differentiation (2nd ed.). SIAM.

Higham, N. J. (2002). Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms (2nd ed.). SIAM.

Stoer, J., & Bulirsch, R. (2002). Introduction to Numerical Analysis (3rd ed.). Springer-Verlag.

3 Chapter 5: Direct Methods for Linear Systems

The solution of linear systems Ax = b forms the computational backbone of scientific computing. From discretized partial differential equations to least squares regression, the ability to solve such systems efficiently and accurately determines the feasibility of countless computational models. This chapter develops the fundamental direct methods that factor the coefficient matrix into simpler forms, enabling systematic solution procedures with predictable computational costs and error bounds.

Direct methods, in contrast to the iterative approaches of Chapter 6, compute the exact solution in a finite number of operations (modulo floating-point errors). They provide the theoretical foundation for understanding when linear systems can be solved and at what computational cost. Moreover, many iterative methods require direct factorizations as preconditioners, making these techniques doubly essential.

3.1 Gaussian Elimination and Pivoting

3.1.1 Forward Elimination

Gaussian elimination systematically transforms the augmented matrix [A|b] into row echelon form through elementary row operations. For an n×n system, we eliminate entries below the diagonal in column k by adding multiples of row k to rows i > k.

Consider the general elimination step. At stage k, we have partially reduced the system to:

[A₁₁  A₁₂  |  b₁]
[0    A₂₂  |  b₂]

where A₁₁ is k×k upper triangular, A₁₂ is k×(n-k), and A₂₂ is (n-k)×(n-k). To eliminate the first column of A₂₂, we compute multipliers mᵢ = A₂₂(i,1)/A₂₂(1,1) for i = 2,…,n-k, then update:

A₂₂(i,:) ← A₂₂(i,:) - mᵢ·A₂₂(1,:) b₂(i) ← b₂(i) - mᵢ·b₂(1)

The process continues until we obtain upper triangular form Ux = c, which we solve by back substitution.

function gaussian_elimination_naive(A, b)
    n = size(A, 1)
    U = copy(A)  # Work with copy to preserve original
    c = copy(b)
    
    # Forward elimination
    for k in 1:n-1
        for i in k+1:n
            # Compute multiplier
            m = U[i,k] / U[k,k]
            # Update row i
            for j in k:n
                U[i,j] -= m * U[k,j]
            end
            c[i] -= m * c[k]
        end
    end
    
    # Back substitution
    x = zeros(n)
    for i in n:-1:1
        x[i] = c[i]
        for j in i+1:n
            x[i] -= U[i,j] * x[j]
        end
        x[i] /= U[i,i]
    end
    
    return x
end

3.1.2 Partial Pivoting

The naive algorithm fails when a diagonal element becomes zero or near-zero, causing division by small numbers that amplify rounding errors. Partial pivoting addresses this by selecting the largest available pivot in each column.

Definition 5.1 (Partial Pivoting). At elimination step k, partial pivoting selects the row index p ≥ k such that |A(p,k)| = max{|A(i,k)| : i = k,…,n}, then swaps rows k and p before elimination.

This strategy ensures that |mᵢₖ| ≤ 1 for all multipliers, providing stability guarantees. The growth factor measures how large matrix entries can become during elimination:

ρ = max{|U(i,j)|} / max{|A(i,j)|}

For partial pivoting, ρ ≤ 2^(n-1), though this bound is rarely achieved in practice.

function gaussian_elimination_pivot(A, b)
    n = size(A, 1)
    U = copy(A)
    c = copy(b)
    p = collect(1:n)  # Permutation vector
    
    # Forward elimination with partial pivoting
    for k in 1:n-1
        # Find pivot
        pivot_row = k + argmax(abs.(U[k:n, k])) - 1
        
        # Swap rows if needed
        if pivot_row != k
            U[[k, pivot_row], :] = U[[pivot_row, k], :]
            c[[k, pivot_row]] = c[[pivot_row, k]]
            p[[k, pivot_row]] = p[[pivot_row, k]]
        end
        
        # Eliminate
        for i in k+1:n
            m = U[i,k] / U[k,k]
            U[i, k:n] -= m * U[k, k:n]
            c[i] -= m * c[k]
        end
    end
    
    # Back substitution (same as before)
    x = zeros(n)
    for i in n:-1:1
        x[i] = c[i] - dot(U[i, i+1:n], x[i+1:n])
        x[i] /= U[i,i]
    end
    
    return x, p
end

3.2 LU Decomposition

3.2.1 Existence and Construction

Rather than working directly with the augmented matrix, we can factor A itself into the product of lower and upper triangular matrices.

Theorem 5.2 (LU Factorization). If Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting applied to A produces upper triangular matrix U, then PA = LU where P is a permutation matrix, L is unit lower triangular (ones on diagonal), and U is upper triangular.

Proof. The key insight is that Gaussian elimination computes the multipliers mᵢₖ that appear in L. Specifically, if no row swaps occur, then L(i,k) = mᵢₖ for i > k and L(i,i) = 1. With pivoting, we must track the permutation matrix P that records all row swaps.

The elimination process can be written as: M_{n-1} P_{n-1} ··· M₁ P₁ A = U

where each Mₖ performs elimination in column k and Pₖ performs the pivoting swap. Since the Mₖ are unit lower triangular and the Pₖ are permutations, we can rearrange to get: PA = LU

where P = P₁ P₂ ··· P_{n-1} and L = M₁⁻¹ M₂⁻¹ ··· M_{n-1}⁻¹. □

3.2.2 Computational Implementation

The LU factorization can be computed in-place, storing L and U in the same n×n array. Since L has ones on the diagonal, we need not store them explicitly.

using LinearAlgebra

function lu_decomposition(A)
    n = size(A, 1)
    LU = copy(A)
    p = collect(1:n)
    
    for k in 1:n-1
        # Find pivot
        pivot_row = k + argmax(abs.(LU[k:n, k])) - 1
        
        # Swap rows
        if pivot_row != k
            LU[[k, pivot_row], :] = LU[[pivot_row, k], :]
            p[[k, pivot_row]] = p[[pivot_row, k]]
        end
        
        # Compute multipliers and store in L part
        for i in k+1:n
            LU[i, k] /= LU[k, k]
        end
        
        # Update remaining submatrix
        for i in k+1:n
            for j in k+1:n
                LU[i, j] -= LU[i, k] * LU[k, j]
            end
        end
    end
    
    return LU, p
end

function solve_lu(LU, p, b)
    n = length(b)
    y = b[p]  # Apply permutation
    
    # Forward substitution: Ly = Pb
    for i in 2:n
        for j in 1:i-1
            y[i] -= LU[i, j] * y[j]
        end
    end
    
    # Back substitution: Ux = y
    x = copy(y)
    for i in n:-1:1
        for j in i+1:n
            x[i] -= LU[i, j] * x[j]
        end
        x[i] /= LU[i, i]
    end
    
    return x
end

3.2.3 Computational Complexity

The operation count for LU decomposition is straightforward to compute. At step k, we perform: - (n-k) divisions to compute multipliers - (n-k)² multiplications and subtractions to update the submatrix

Summing over k = 1 to n-1: ∑ₖ₌₁ⁿ⁻¹ (n-k)² = ∑ⱼ₌₁ⁿ⁻¹ j² = (n-1)n(2n-1)/6 ≈ n³/3

Including the forward and back substitution steps (each O(n²)), the total cost is (2/3)n³ + O(n²) operations.

Theorem 5.3 (LU Complexity). LU decomposition of an n×n matrix requires (2/3)n³ + O(n²) floating-point operations. Solving additional systems with the same coefficient matrix costs only 2n² operations per right-hand side.

This factorization-based approach becomes increasingly advantageous when solving multiple systems with the same A but different b vectors, a common scenario in time-stepping algorithms and parameter studies.

3.3 Cholesky Decomposition

3.3.1 Symmetric Positive Definite Systems

When the coefficient matrix possesses additional structure, specialized algorithms can exploit this to reduce computational cost and improve numerical stability. The most important case is symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices.

Definition 5.4 (Symmetric Positive Definite). A matrix A ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ is symmetric positive definite if A = Aᵀ and xᵀAx > 0 for all x ≠ 0.

SPD matrices arise naturally in many applications: discretizations of elliptic PDEs with Dirichlet boundary conditions, normal equations AᵀAx = Aᵀb in least squares, and covariance matrices in statistics. For such matrices, we can compute a specialized factorization.

Theorem 5.5 (Cholesky Factorization). Every symmetric positive definite matrix A has a unique factorization A = LLᵀ where L is lower triangular with positive diagonal entries.

Proof. We prove existence by construction. The algorithm proceeds by computing L column by column. Suppose we have computed the first k-1 columns of L. The (k,k) entry satisfies:

A(k,k) = ∑ⱼ₌₁ᵏ L(k,j)² = ∑ⱼ₌₁ᵏ⁻¹ L(k,j)² + L(k,k)²

Thus L(k,k) = √(A(k,k) - ∑ⱼ₌₁ᵏ⁻¹ L(k,j)²). Positive definiteness ensures this quantity is positive. For i > k:

A(i,k) = ∑ⱼ₌₁ᵏ L(i,j)L(k,j) = ∑ⱼ₌₁ᵏ⁻¹ L(i,j)L(k,j) + L(i,k)L(k,k)

Solving for L(i,k) gives the algorithm. Uniqueness follows from the requirement that L have positive diagonal entries. □

3.3.2 Cholesky Algorithm

The Cholesky algorithm computes L directly without pivoting (none is needed for SPD matrices).

function cholesky_decomposition(A)
    n = size(A, 1)
    L = zeros(n, n)
    
    for k in 1:n
        # Compute L[k,k]
        sum_sq = sum(L[k, 1:k-1].^2)
        L[k, k] = sqrt(A[k, k] - sum_sq)
        
        # Compute L[i,k] for i > k
        for i in k+1:n
            sum_prod = dot(L[i, 1:k-1], L[k, 1:k-1])
            L[i, k] = (A[i, k] - sum_prod) / L[k, k]
        end
    end
    
    return L
end

function solve_cholesky(L, b)
    n = length(b)
    
    # Forward substitution: Ly = b
    y = zeros(n)
    for i in 1:n
        y[i] = b[i] - dot(L[i, 1:i-1], y[1:i-1])
        y[i] /= L[i, i]
    end
    
    # Back substitution: L^T x = y
    x = zeros(n)
    for i in n:-1:1
        x[i] = y[i] - dot(L[i+1:n, i], x[i+1:n])
        x[i] /= L[i, i]
    end
    
    return x
end

The operation count for Cholesky decomposition is approximately half that of LU:

∑ₖ₌₁ⁿ [(n-k+1) + (n-k)] ≈ n³/3

This factor-of-two savings, combined with the lack of pivoting overhead, makes Cholesky the method of choice for SPD systems.

3.4 Banded Systems

3.4.1 Tridiagonal Systems

Many discretizations produce banded coefficient matrices, where nonzero entries are confined to a few diagonals near the main diagonal. The most common case is tridiagonal matrices arising from one-dimensional finite difference schemes.

Definition 5.6 (Tridiagonal Matrix). A matrix A ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ is tridiagonal if A(i,j) = 0 whenever |i-j| > 1.

For such matrices, we can represent the three diagonals compactly and perform elimination without computing entries known to be zero.

function solve_tridiagonal(a, b, c, d)
    # a: subdiagonal, b: diagonal, c: superdiagonal, d: right-hand side
    n = length(b)
    
    # Modify coefficients in place (working arrays)
    b_work = copy(b)
    d_work = copy(d)
    
    # Forward elimination
    for i in 2:n
        m = a[i-1] / b_work[i-1]
        b_work[i] -= m * c[i-1]
        d_work[i] -= m * d_work[i-1]
    end
    
    # Back substitution
    x = zeros(n)
    x[n] = d_work[n] / b_work[n]
    for i in n-1:-1:1
        x[i] = (d_work[i] - c[i] * x[i+1]) / b_work[i]
    end
    
    return x
end

The algorithm requires only O(n) operations—a dramatic improvement over the O(n³) cost of general methods. This efficiency enables the solution of very large one-dimensional problems.

3.4.2 General Banded Systems

For matrices with bandwidth 2p+1 (p super- and sub-diagonals), Gaussian elimination preserves the band structure, requiring O(p²n) operations. The LinearAlgebra package provides efficient implementations:

using LinearAlgebra

# Example: pentadiagonal system (p=2)
function demo_banded_system()
    n = 1000
    A = Tridiagonal(ones(n-1), 4*ones(n), ones(n-1))  # Simple tridiagonal
    b = randn(n)
    
    # Direct solution
    @time x = A \ b
    
    # For comparison with general solver
    A_dense = Matrix(A)
    @time x_dense = A_dense \ b
    
    println("Residual: ", norm(A*x - b))
    return x
end

3.5 QR Factorization via Householder Reflectors

3.5.1 Geometric Motivation

The QR factorization represents a matrix as the product of an orthogonal matrix Q and an upper triangular matrix R. Unlike LU decomposition, QR factorization exists for all matrices and provides excellent numerical stability.

The key insight is to use orthogonal transformations that introduce zeros below the diagonal one column at a time. Householder reflectors provide the most stable approach.

Definition 5.7 (Householder Reflector). For a nonzero vector v ∈ ℝⁿ, the Householder reflector is H = I - 2vvᵀ/||v||₂².

Householder reflectors are symmetric, orthogonal, and satisfy H² = I. Given a vector x, we can choose v such that Hx = αe₁ for some scalar α, effectively reflecting x onto the first coordinate axis.

3.5.2 Construction Algorithm

To annihilate entries below x₁, we choose: v = x ± ||x||₂e₁

The sign is selected to avoid cancellation: use the opposite sign of x₁.

function householder_vector(x)
    n = length(x)
    v = copy(x)
    
    # Choose sign to avoid cancellation
    sigma = sign(x[1]) * norm(x)
    v[1] += sigma
    
    # Normalize
    beta = 2 / dot(v, v)
    
    return v, beta
end

function apply_householder!(A, v, beta, k)
    # Apply H = I - beta*v*v^T to columns k:end of A
    m, n = size(A)
    for j in k:n
        s = beta * dot(v, A[k:m, j])
        A[k:m, j] -= s * v
    end
end

function qr_householder(A)
    m, n = size(A)
    R = copy(A)
    Q = Matrix{Float64}(I, m, m)
    
    for k in 1:min(m-1, n)
        # Generate Householder vector for column k
        x = R[k:m, k]
        v, beta = householder_vector(x)
        
        # Apply to remaining columns of R
        apply_householder!(R, v, beta, k)
        
        # Apply to Q (accumulate transformations)
        Q_temp = Q[:, k:m]
        for i in 1:m
            s = beta * dot(v, Q_temp[i, :])
            Q_temp[i, :] -= s * v'
        end
        Q[:, k:m] = Q_temp
    end
    
    return Q, R[1:min(m,n), :]
end

3.5.3 Alternative: Economy QR

When m > n (tall matrices), we often need only the first n columns of Q, yielding the “economy” or “thin” QR factorization A = Q̂R̂ where Q̂ ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ and R̂ ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ.

function qr_economy(A)
    m, n = size(A)
    @assert m >= n "Economy QR requires m >= n"
    
    R = copy(A)
    Q = zeros(m, n)
    
    for k in 1:n
        # Generate Householder vector
        x = R[k:m, k]
        v, beta = householder_vector(x)
        
        # Apply to R
        for j in k:n
            s = beta * dot(v, R[k:m, j])
            R[k:m, j] -= s * v
        end
        
        # Store Q column
        q_k = zeros(m)
        q_k[k] = 1.0
        q_k[k:m] -= beta * dot(v, q_k[k:m]) * v
        Q[:, k] = q_k
    end
    
    return Q, R[1:n, :]
end

3.6 Least Squares via QR and SVD

3.6.1 The Overdetermined Case

When A ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ with m > n, the system Ax = b typically has no exact solution. We seek instead the least squares solution that minimizes ||Ax - b||₂.

Theorem 5.8 (Normal Equations). The least squares solution to Ax = b satisfies AᵀAx = Aᵀb. If A has full column rank, the solution is unique: x = (AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb.

While theoretically correct, forming AᵀA squares the condition number of A, potentially causing numerical difficulties. QR factorization provides a more stable approach.

3.6.2 QR-Based Least Squares

If A = QR with Q ∈ ℝᵐˣᵐ orthogonal and R ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ upper triangular, then:

||Ax - b||₂² = ||QRx - b||₂² = ||Rx - Qᵀb||₂²

Partitioning Q = [Q₁ Q₂] where Q₁ ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ and R = [R₁; 0] where R₁ ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ:

||Ax - b||₂² = ||R₁x - Q₁ᵀb||₂² + ||Q₂ᵀb||₂²

Since the second term is independent of x, we minimize by setting R₁x = Q₁ᵀb.

function least_squares_qr(A, b)
    m, n = size(A)
    @assert m >= n "Requires overdetermined system"
    
    # Economy QR factorization
    Q, R = qr(A)
    Q_reduced = Q[:, 1:n]
    R_reduced = R[1:n, :]
    
    # Solve R x = Q^T b
    rhs = Q_reduced' * b
    x = R_reduced \ rhs
    
    # Compute residual
    residual_norm = norm(b - A*x)
    
    return x, residual_norm
end

# Example: polynomial fitting
function demo_polynomial_fit()
    # Generate noisy data
    t = 0:0.1:2π
    y_true = sin.(t)
    y_data = y_true + 0.1*randn(length(t))
    
    # Fit polynomial of degree 5
    degree = 5
    A = hcat([t.^k for k in 0:degree]...)
    
    coeffs, residual = least_squares_qr(A, y_data)
    
    # Evaluate fit
    y_fit = A * coeffs
    
    println("Polynomial coefficients: ", coeffs)
    println("Residual norm: ", residual)
    
    return coeffs, t, y_data, y_fit
end

3.6.3 SVD-Based Least Squares

For rank-deficient matrices or when the rank is uncertain, the singular value decomposition provides the most robust approach.

Definition 5.9 (Singular Value Decomposition). Every matrix A ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ has a factorization A = UΣVᵀ where U ∈ ℝᵐˣᵐ and V ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ are orthogonal, and Σ ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ is diagonal with nonnegative entries σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ … ≥ σₘᵢₙ ≥ 0.

The SVD-based least squares solution handles rank deficiency by computing the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse:

using LinearAlgebra

function least_squares_svd(A, b; tol=1e-12)
    U, σ, V = svd(A)
    
    # Determine effective rank
    rank_A = sum(σ .> tol * σ[1])
    
    # Pseudoinverse solution
    x = zeros(size(A, 2))
    for i in 1:rank_A
        x += (dot(U[:, i], b) / σ[i]) * V[:, i]
    end
    
    residual_norm = norm(b - A*x)
    
    return x, residual_norm, rank_A
end

3.7 Sparse Direct Solvers

3.7.1 Storage Formats

Real applications often involve sparse matrices where most entries are zero. Storing these matrices in dense format wastes memory and computational effort. Several compressed formats address this issue.

The Compressed Sparse Column (CSC) format stores three arrays: - colptr: column pointer array indicating where each column starts - rowval: row indices of nonzero entries
- nzval: numerical values of nonzero entries

using SparseArrays

function demo_sparse_formats()
    # Create a sparse tridiagonal matrix
    n = 1000
    I = [1:n-1; 1:n; 2:n]  # Row indices
    J = [2:n; 1:n; 1:n-1]  # Column indices  
    V = [ones(n-1); 4*ones(n); ones(n-1)]  # Values
    
    A = sparse(I, J, V, n, n)
    
    println("Sparsity: ", nnz(A), " / ", n^2, " = ", nnz(A)/n^2)
    println("Storage format: ", typeof(A))
    
    # Access pattern matters for sparse matrices
    b = randn(n)
    
    @time x = A \ b  # Sparse solver
    @time x_dense = Matrix(A) \ b  # Dense solver
    
    println("Residual: ", norm(A*x - b))
    return A, x
end

3.7.2 Fill-in and Reordering

Gaussian elimination on sparse matrices can create nonzeros (fill-in) where the original matrix had zeros. The ordering of variables significantly affects the amount of fill-in.

function demo_fill_in()
    # Create 2D grid Laplacian (5-point stencil)
    function grid_laplacian(nx, ny)
        n = nx * ny
        I = Int[]
        J = Int[]
        V = Float64[]
        
        for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny
            idx = (j-1)*nx + i
            
            # Center point
            push!(I, idx); push!(J, idx); push!(V, 4.0)
            
            # Left neighbor
            if i > 1
                push!(I, idx); push!(J, idx-1); push!(V, -1.0)
            end
            # Right neighbor  
            if i < nx
                push!(I, idx); push!(J, idx+1); push!(V, -1.0)
            end
            # Bottom neighbor
            if j > 1
                push!(I, idx); push!(J, idx-nx); push!(V, -1.0)
            end
            # Top neighbor
            if j < ny
                push!(I, idx); push!(J, idx+nx); push!(V, -1.0)
            end
        end
        
        return sparse(I, J, V, n, n)
    end
    
    A = grid_laplacian(20, 20)
    b = randn(400)
    
    # Natural ordering vs reordering will show different fill-in
    println("Original nnz: ", nnz(A))
    
    # Solve (Julia automatically chooses good algorithms)
    @time x = A \ b
    
    return A, x
end

3.7.3 Direct Solver Workflow

Modern sparse direct solvers follow a general pattern: 1. Analyze: Determine sparsity pattern and optimal elimination order 2. Factor: Perform numerical LU or Cholesky factorization
3. Solve: Forward/backward substitution for each right-hand side

function sparse_solver_demo()
    # Create a larger sparse system
    n = 1000
    A = sprand(n, n, 0.01) + I  # Random sparse + identity for stability
    A = A + A'  # Make symmetric positive definite
    b = randn(n)
    
    # Time the solution process
    println("Solving sparse system of size ", n)
    @time x = A \ b
    
    # For multiple right-hand sides, factorization can be reused
    B = randn(n, 10)  # Multiple RHS
    @time X = A \ B
    
    println("Residual (single RHS): ", norm(A*x - b))
    println("Residual (multiple RHS): ", norm(A*X - B))
    
    return A, x, X
end

The Julia LinearAlgebra and SparseArrays packages automatically select appropriate algorithms based on matrix properties, including specialized routines for symmetric, positive definite, and triangular matrices.

3.8 Worked Examples

3.8.1 Example 5.1: Finite Difference Heat Equation

Consider the one-dimensional heat equation u_t = u_xx on [0,1] with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions. Using backward Euler time stepping and centered finite differences in space:

(I - rA)u^(n+1) = u^n

where r = Δt/(Δx)² and A is the discrete Laplacian.

function heat_equation_1d()
    # Parameters
    L = 1.0  # Domain length
    T = 0.1  # Final time
    nx = 99  # Interior points
    dx = L / (nx + 1)
    dt = 0.001
    nt = Int(T / dt)
    r = dt / dx^2
    
    # Discrete Laplacian (tridiagonal)
    A = Tridiagonal(-ones(nx-1), 2*ones(nx), -ones(nx-1))
    
    # System matrix
    M = I - r*A
    
    # Initial condition: u(x,0) = sin(π*x)
    x = LinRange(dx, L-dx, nx)
    u = sin.(π * x)
    
    # Time stepping
    for n in 1:nt
        u = M \ u  # Solve linear system each time step
    end
    
    # Analytical solution for comparison
    u_exact = exp(-π^2 * T) * sin.(π * x)
    
    println("Max error: ", maximum(abs.(u - u_exact)))
    
    return x, u, u_exact
end

3.8.2 Example 5.2: Least Squares Polynomial Fitting with Cross-Validation

Demonstrate polynomial fitting with model selection via cross-validation:

function polynomial_cross_validation()
    # Generate synthetic data with noise
    n_data = 50
    x_data = sort(2π * rand(n_data))
    y_true = 0.5*sin(2*x_data) + 0.3*cos(3*x_data)
    σ_noise = 0.1
    y_data = y_true + σ_noise * randn(n_data)
    
    # Test polynomial degrees 1 through 10
    max_degree = 10
    n_folds = 5
    cv_errors = zeros(max_degree)
    
    # K-fold cross-validation
    fold_size = div(n_data, n_folds)
    
    for degree in 1:max_degree
        fold_errors = zeros(n_folds)
        
        for fold


\newpage

# Chapter 6: Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

The direct methods studied in Chapter 5 provide exact solutions to linear systems Ax = b (up to roundoff error) in a finite number of operations. However, when A is large and sparse, iterative methods often prove more efficient in both computational time and memory usage. Rather than factoring A, iterative methods construct a sequence of approximations {x^(k)} that converges to the true solution x*.

This chapter develops the theory and practice of iterative methods, beginning with classical stationary iterations and progressing to modern Krylov subspace methods. We establish convergence conditions through spectral analysis and explore preconditioning strategies that accelerate convergence for challenging problems.

## Classical Iterative Methods

Classical iterative methods, also called stationary methods, generate the sequence x^(k+1) = Gx^(k) + c for some iteration matrix G and vector c that remain constant throughout the iteration.

### Matrix Splitting and Basic Iterations

Every iterative method can be viewed as a matrix splitting of A.

> **Definition 6.1 (Matrix Splitting).** A splitting of matrix A is a decomposition A = M - N where M is nonsingular. The associated iterative method is x^(k+1) = M^(-1)Nx^(k) + M^(-1)b.

The iteration matrix is G = M^(-1)N = I - M^(-1)A. For the method to converge, we require that the spectral radius ρ(G) < 1, where ρ(G) = max{|λ| : λ is an eigenvalue of G}.

The three classical methods correspond to different choices of M:

**Jacobi Method**: M = D (diagonal of A), N = D - A = -(L + U)
**Gauss-Seidel Method**: M = D + L (lower triangular), N = -U  
**Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR)**: M = (1/ω)D + L, N = (1/ω - 1)D - U

where A = D + L + U with D diagonal, L strictly lower triangular, and U strictly upper triangular.

### The Jacobi Method

The Jacobi method is the simplest iterative scheme. From Ax = b, we solve the i-th equation for x_i:

x_i^(k+1) = (b_i - Σ_{j≠i} a_{ij}x_j^(k))/a_{ii}

This requires all diagonal entries a_{ii} ≠ 0.

> **Example 6.1 (Jacobi Iteration).** Consider the system:
> ```
> 4x₁ - x₂ + x₃ = 4
> x₁ + 4x₂ - x₃ = 6  
> -x₁ + x₂ + 4x₃ = 2
> ```
> The Jacobi iteration becomes:
> ```
> x₁^(k+1) = (4 + x₂^(k) - x₃^(k))/4
> x₂^(k+1) = (6 - x₁^(k) + x₃^(k))/4  
> x₃^(k+1) = (2 + x₁^(k) - x₂^(k))/4
> ```

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

function jacobi!(x, A, b, maxiter=100, tol=1e-10)
    n = length(b)
    x_new = similar(x)
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        # Extract diagonal values for efficiency
        for i in 1:n
            sum_val = 0.0
            for j in 1:n
                if i != j
                    sum_val += A[i,j] * x[j]
                end
            end
            x_new[i] = (b[i] - sum_val) / A[i,i]
        end
        
        # Check convergence
        if norm(x_new - x, Inf) < tol
            println("Jacobi converged in $iter iterations")
            return copy!(x, x_new), iter
        end
        
        copy!(x, x_new)
    end
    
    error("Jacobi failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Test the example system
A = [4.0 -1.0 1.0; 1.0 4.0 -1.0; -1.0 1.0 4.0]
b = [4.0, 6.0, 2.0]
x0 = zeros(3)

x_jacobi, iterations = jacobi!(copy(x0), A, b)
println("Solution: ", x_jacobi)
println("Residual: ", norm(A*x_jacobi - b))

3.8.3 The Gauss-Seidel Method

The Gauss-Seidel method uses updated values as soon as they become available:

x_i^(k+1) = (b_i - Σ_{j<i} a_{ij}x_j^(k+1) - Σ_{j>i} a_{ij}x_j^(k))/a_{ii}

This typically converges faster than Jacobi when both converge, as it uses more current information.

function gauss_seidel!(x, A, b, maxiter=100, tol=1e-10)
    n = length(b)
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        x_old = copy(x)
        
        for i in 1:n
            sum_val = 0.0
            # Use updated values for j < i
            for j in 1:(i-1)
                sum_val += A[i,j] * x[j]  # Already updated
            end
            # Use old values for j > i  
            for j in (i+1):n
                sum_val += A[i,j] * x[j]  # Not yet updated
            end
            x[i] = (b[i] - sum_val) / A[i,i]
        end
        
        if norm(x - x_old, Inf) < tol
            println("Gauss-Seidel converged in $iter iterations")
            return x, iter
        end
    end
    
    error("Gauss-Seidel failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

x_gs, iter_gs = gauss_seidel!(copy(x0), A, b)
println("Gauss-Seidel solution: ", x_gs)
println("Gauss-Seidel iterations: ", iter_gs)

3.8.4 Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR)

SOR introduces a relaxation parameter ω to potentially accelerate convergence:

x_i^(k+1) = (1-ω)x_i^(k) + (ω/a_{ii})(b_i - Σ_{j<i} a_{ij}x_j^(k+1) - Σ_{j>i} a_{ij}x_j^(k))

When ω = 1, SOR reduces to Gauss-Seidel. The optimal choice of ω depends on the spectral properties of the iteration matrix.

Theorem 6.1 (SOR Convergence). For SOR to converge, it is necessary that 0 < ω < 2. For symmetric positive definite matrices, SOR converges for any 0 < ω < 2.

Proof. The eigenvalues λ of the SOR iteration matrix satisfy the characteristic equation relating them to the Jacobi eigenvalues μ: (λ + ω - 1)² = λω²μ². For convergence, |λ| < 1. When A is symmetric positive definite, the Jacobi eigenvalues are real and |μ| < 1, and one can show that choosing ω ∈ (0,2) ensures |λ| < 1. □

function sor!(x, A, b, ω, maxiter=100, tol=1e-10)
    n = length(b)
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        x_old = copy(x)
        
        for i in 1:n
            sum_val = 0.0
            for j in 1:(i-1)
                sum_val += A[i,j] * x[j]
            end
            for j in (i+1):n
                sum_val += A[i,j] * x[j]
            end
            
            # SOR update
            x_new = (b[i] - sum_val) / A[i,i]
            x[i] = (1 - ω) * x[i] + ω * x_new
        end
        
        if norm(x - x_old, Inf) < tol
            println("SOR(ω=$ω) converged in $iter iterations")
            return x, iter
        end
    end
    
    error("SOR failed to converge in $maxiter iterations")
end

# Test different ω values
for ω in [0.5, 1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8]
    x_sor, iter_sor = sor!(copy(x0), A, b, ω)
    println("ω = $ω: $iter_sor iterations")
end

3.8.5 Convergence Analysis via Spectral Radius

The convergence rate of stationary iterations is determined by the spectral radius of the iteration matrix.

Theorem 6.2 (Convergence of Stationary Iterations). The iterative method x^(k+1) = Gx^(k) + c converges for any starting vector x^(0) if and only if ρ(G) < 1. The error satisfies ||e^(k)|| ≤ ||G||^k ||e^(0)|| for any matrix norm, and the asymptotic convergence rate is -ln(ρ(G)).

The number of iterations required to reduce the error by a factor ε is approximately -ln(ε)/ln(ρ(G)). This shows why reducing the spectral radius is crucial for fast convergence.

For the Jacobi method applied to diagonally dominant matrices, we have the following important result:

Theorem 6.3 (Jacobi Convergence for Diagonal Dominance). If A is strictly diagonally dominant (|a_{ii}| > Σ_{j≠i} |a_{ij}| for all i), then the Jacobi method converges.

Proof. The iteration matrix G = -D^(-1)(L+U) has entries |G_{ij}| = |a_{ij}/a_{ii}| for i ≠ j. The row sums satisfy Σ_j |G_{ij}| = Σ_{j≠i} |a_{ij}|/|a_{ii}| < 1 by diagonal dominance. By the Gershgorin circle theorem, all eigenvalues lie within unit discs, so ρ(G) < 1. □

3.9 Krylov Subspace Methods

While classical methods are simple to implement and analyze, they often converge slowly for large systems. Krylov subspace methods achieve much faster convergence by working in carefully constructed subspaces that capture the essential behavior of the linear system.

3.9.1 The Krylov Subspace

Definition 6.2 (Krylov Subspace). For matrix A ∈ ℝ^(n×n) and vector v ∈ ℝ^n, the m-th Krylov subspace is K_m(A,v) = span{v, Av, A²v, …, A^(m-1)v}.

The vectors {v, Av, A²v, …} form a Krylov sequence. These vectors generally become linearly dependent after at most n steps, at which point K_n(A,v) = K_m(A,v) for all m ≥ n.

Krylov methods construct approximations x_m ∈ x_0 + K_m(A,r_0), where r_0 = b - Ax_0 is the initial residual. Different methods use different criteria to select the “best” approximation within this subspace.

3.9.2 The Conjugate Gradient Method

For symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices, the conjugate gradient (CG) method provides the optimal Krylov subspace method. CG minimizes the A-norm of the error over the Krylov subspace.

Definition 6.3 (A-norm). For SPD matrix A, the A-norm (or energy norm) is ||v||_A = √(v^T A v).

The key insight is that minimizing ||x - x_m||_A is equivalent to minimizing the quadratic form φ(x) = (1/2)x^T A x - b^T x over x_0 + K_m(A,r_0).

Theorem 6.4 (CG Optimality). The CG iterate x_m minimizes ||x - x_m||_A over all x_m ∈ x_0 + K_m(A,r_0).

The algorithm constructs A-orthogonal (conjugate) search directions {p_0, p_1, …} such that p_i^T A p_j = 0 for i ≠ j. The construction uses three-term recurrences that maintain orthogonality while avoiding storage of all previous vectors.

function conjugate_gradient(A, b, x0=zeros(length(b)); 
                           maxiter=length(b), tol=1e-10, verbose=false)
    n = length(b)
    x = copy(x0)
    r = b - A * x  # Initial residual
    p = copy(r)    # Initial search direction
    rsold = dot(r, r)
    
    residuals = [sqrt(rsold)]
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        Ap = A * p
        α = rsold / dot(p, Ap)  # Step size
        x .+= α .* p            # Update solution
        r .-= α .* Ap           # Update residual
        
        rsnew = dot(r, r)
        residual_norm = sqrt(rsnew)
        push!(residuals, residual_norm)
        
        if verbose && iter % 10 == 0
            println("CG iteration $iter: residual = $residual_norm")
        end
        
        if residual_norm < tol
            if verbose
                println("CG converged in $iter iterations")
            end
            return x, residuals[1:iter+1]
        end
        
        β = rsnew / rsold      # Improvement factor
        p .= r .+ β .* p       # New search direction
        rsold = rsnew
    end
    
    @warn "CG reached maximum iterations ($maxiter)"
    return x, residuals
end

# Test CG on a SPD system
using Random
Random.seed!(123)

# Create a SPD test matrix
n = 100
A_base = randn(n, n)
A_spd = A_base' * A_base + 5*I  # Ensure positive definiteness
b_test = randn(n)
x_true = A_spd \ b_test

# Solve with CG
x_cg, residuals = conjugate_gradient(A_spd, b_test, verbose=true)
println("CG error: ", norm(x_cg - x_true))
println("Final residual: ", residuals[end])

3.9.3 CG Convergence Theory

The convergence rate of CG depends on the condition number κ(A) = λ_max/λ_min.

Theorem 6.5 (CG Convergence Rate). For the conjugate gradient method applied to SPD matrix A, the error in the A-norm satisfies ||x - x_m||_A ≤ 2||x - x_0||_A ((√κ - 1)/(√κ + 1))^m

This shows that CG converges exponentially with rate depending on √κ rather than κ itself—a significant improvement over stationary methods for well-conditioned problems.

The bound implies that the number of iterations to reduce the error by ε is approximately (√κ/2)ln(2/ε), which is much better than the κ ln(1/ε) bound for stationary methods.

Example 6.2 (CG vs. Jacobi Performance). For a matrix with κ(A) = 100, CG requires roughly √100/2 = 5 times ln(2/10^(-6)) ≈ 75 iterations, while Jacobi needs approximately 100 × ln(10^6) ≈ 1380 iterations.

# Compare CG and Jacobi on a range of condition numbers
function compare_methods()
    results = []
    
    for κ in [10, 100, 1000]
        n = 50
        # Construct matrix with known condition number
        λ = range(1, κ, n)  # Eigenvalues from 1 to κ
        Q, _ = qr(randn(n, n))
        A = Q * Diagonal(λ) * Q'  # A = Q Λ Q^T
        b = randn(n)
        
        # CG solve
        x_cg, residuals_cg = conjugate_gradient(A, b, tol=1e-8)
        iter_cg = length(residuals_cg) - 1
        
        # Jacobi solve (if it converges)
        try
            x_jac, iter_jac = jacobi!(zeros(n), A, b, 2000, 1e-8)
            push!(results, (κ, iter_cg, iter_jac))
        catch
            push!(results, (κ, iter_cg, "diverged"))
        end
    end
    
    for (κ, cg_iter, jac_iter) in results
        println("κ = $κ: CG = $cg_iter, Jacobi = $jac_iter")
    end
end

compare_methods()

3.9.4 GMRES for General Systems

When A is not symmetric positive definite, CG may fail or converge slowly. The Generalized Minimal Residual (GMRES) method extends Krylov subspace techniques to general matrices by minimizing the residual norm ||b - Ax_m||₂.

GMRES uses the Arnoldi process to build an orthonormal basis for the Krylov subspace. After m steps, we have AV_m = V_{m+1}H_m, where V_m has orthonormal columns spanning K_m(A,r_0), and H_m is upper Hessenberg.

Definition 6.4 (Arnoldi Process). The Arnoldi process constructs an orthonormal basis {v₁, v₂, …, v_m} for K_m(A,v₁) via modified Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization: 1. v₁ = r₀/||r₀||₂ 2. For j = 1,2,…,m: - w = Av_j - For i = 1,…,j: h_{i,j} = ⟨w, v_i⟩, w ← w - h_{i,j}v_i
- h_{j+1,j} = ||w||₂, v_{j+1} = w/h_{j+1,j}

The GMRES approximation minimizes ||b - Ax||₂ over x ∈ x₀ + K_m(A,r₀), which reduces to solving the least squares problem min_y ||β e₁ - H_m y||₂, where β = ||r₀||₂ and e₁ is the first unit vector.

function gmres(A, b, x0=zeros(length(b)); 
               maxiter=min(length(b), 100), tol=1e-10, verbose=false)
    n = length(b)
    x = copy(x0)
    r = b - A * x
    β = norm(r)
    
    if β < tol
        return x, [β]
    end
    
    # Allocate storage for Arnoldi vectors and Hessenberg matrix
    V = zeros(n, maxiter + 1)
    H = zeros(maxiter + 1, maxiter)
    V[:, 1] = r / β
    
    residuals = [β]
    
    for m in 1:maxiter
        # Arnoldi step
        w = A * V[:, m]
        
        # Modified Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization
        for i in 1:m
            H[i, m] = dot(w, V[:, i])
            w -= H[i, m] * V[:, i]
        end
        
        H[m+1, m] = norm(w)
        
        if H[m+1, m] < tol  # Lucky breakdown
            V[:, m+1] = zeros(n)
        else
            V[:, m+1] = w / H[m+1, m]
        end
        
        # Solve least squares problem min ||β e₁ - H_m y||₂
        e1 = zeros(m+1)
        e1[1] = β
        
        # Use QR factorization of H[1:m+1, 1:m]
        H_m = H[1:m+1, 1:m]
        y = H_m \ e1  # Solve in least squares sense
        
        # Update solution
        x_new = x0 + V[:, 1:m] * y
        
        # Compute residual norm
        residual_norm = norm(b - A * x_new)
        push!(residuals, residual_norm)
        
        if verbose && m % 5 == 0
            println("GMRES iteration $m: residual = $residual_norm")
        end
        
        if residual_norm < tol
            if verbose
                println("GMRES converged in $m iterations")
            end
            return x_new, residuals
        end
        
        x = x_new
    end
    
    @warn "GMRES reached maximum iterations ($maxiter)"
    return x, residuals
end

# Test GMRES on a non-symmetric system
A_nonsym = randn(50, 50) + 0.1*I  # Avoid singularity
b_nonsym = randn(50)

x_gmres, residuals_gmres = gmres(A_nonsym, b_nonsym, verbose=true)
println("GMRES final residual: ", residuals_gmres[end])

3.9.5 Restarted GMRES

Full GMRES requires storing all Arnoldi vectors, leading to increasing memory and computational costs. Restarted GMRES limits the Krylov subspace dimension to m and restarts with the current approximation.

function gmres_restart(A, b, x0=zeros(length(b)); 
                      restart=20, maxiter=100, tol=1e-10, verbose=false)
    x = copy(x0)
    overall_residuals = [norm(b - A*x)]
    
    for restart_iter in 1:div(maxiter, restart)
        # Run GMRES for 'restart' iterations
        x_new, residuals = gmres(A, b, x, maxiter=restart, tol=tol)
        
        append!(overall_residuals, residuals[2:end])  # Skip duplicate initial residual
        x = x_new
        
        if overall_residuals[end] < tol
            if verbose
                total_iters = length(overall_residuals) - 1
                println("Restarted GMRES converged in $total_iters iterations")
            end
            break
        end
        
        if verbose
            println("Restart $restart_iter: residual = $(overall_residuals[end])")
        end
    end
    
    return x, overall_residuals
end

3.10 Preconditioning

Preconditioning transforms the linear system to improve the spectral properties and accelerate convergence. Instead of solving Ax = b, we solve the equivalent system PAx = Pb or AP⁻¹u = b (where x = P⁻¹u) for some nonsingular preconditioning matrix P.

3.10.1 Preconditioning Strategies

Definition 6.5 (Preconditioner). A preconditioner P for matrix A is a nonsingular matrix such that P⁻¹A (or AP⁻¹) has better spectral properties than A. Ideally, P ≈ A but P⁻¹ is cheap to compute.

The effectiveness of a preconditioner depends on two competing factors: 1. Quality: How well P⁻¹A clusters eigenvalues around 1 2. Efficiency: How cheaply we can solve Py = z

Common preconditioning strategies include:

Diagonal (Jacobi) Preconditioning: P = diag(A). Simple but often ineffective.

Incomplete LU (ILU) Preconditioning: Compute approximate LU factors by dropping small entries during factorization.

Multigrid: Use solutions on coarser grids to accelerate fine-grid convergence.

3.10.2 Incomplete LU Preconditioning

ILU factorization performs Gaussian elimination but maintains sparsity by dropping fill-in elements below a threshold.

using SparseArrays

function ilu_preconditioner(A, τ=1e-3)
    # Simple ILU(0) - only keep original sparsity pattern
    n = size(A, 1)
    L = sparse(tril(A, -1))  # Lower part without diagonal
    U = sparse(triu(A))      # Upper part with diagonal
    
    # ILU factorization maintaining sparsity pattern
    for k in 1:(n-1)
        for i in (k+1):n
            if L[i, k] != 0  # Only if entry exists in original pattern
                L[i, k] /= U[k, k]
                for j in (k+1):n
                    if U[k, j] != 0 && U[i, j] != 0  # Only existing entries
                        U[i, j] -= L[i, k] * U[k, j]
                    end
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    # Add identity to L diagonal
    L += sparse(I, n, n)
    
    return L, U
end

# Preconditioned conjugate gradient
function pcg(A, b, P_solve; x0=zeros(length(b)), maxiter=length(b), tol=1e-10)
    x = copy(x0)
    r = b - A * x
    z = P_solve(r)  # Solve Pz = r
    p = copy(z)
    rzold = dot(r, z)
    
    residuals = [norm(r)]
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        Ap = A * p
        α = rzold / dot(p, Ap)
        x += α * p
        r -= α * Ap
        
        residual_norm = norm(r)
        push!(residuals, residual_norm)
        
        if residual_norm < tol
            println("PCG converged in $iter iterations")
            return x, residuals
        end
        
        z = P_solve(r)
        rznew = dot(r, z)
        β = rznew / rzold
        p = z + β * p
        rzold = rznew
    end
    
    return x, residuals
end

# Test ILU preconditioning
function test_preconditioning()
    # Create a test problem (discrete Laplacian)
    n = 50
    A = spdiagm(-1 => -ones(n-1), 0 => 4*ones(n), 1 => -ones(n-1))
    b = ones(n)
    
    println("Testing preconditioning on $(n)×$(n) system")
    println("Condition number: ", cond(Matrix(A)))
    
    # Unpreconditioned CG
    x_cg, res_cg = conjugate_gradient(A, b)
    println("CG iterations: ", length(res_cg)-1)
    
    # ILU preconditioned CG
    L, U = ilu_preconditioner(A)
    P_solve = z -> U \ (L \ z)  # Solve LUy = z
    
    x_pcg, res_pcg = pcg(A, b, P_solve)
    println("PCG iterations: ", length(res_pcg)-1)
    
    return length(res_cg)-1, length(res_pcg)-1
end

cg_iters, pcg_iters = test_preconditioning()
println("Speedup factor: ", cg_iters / pcg_iters)

3.11 When to Use Iterative vs. Direct Methods

The choice between iterative and direct methods depends on several problem characteristics:

3.11.1 Problem Size and Sparsity

Direct methods are preferred when: - n ≤ 10,000 and the matrix is not extremely sparse - Multiple right-hand sides need to be solved - High accuracy is required (near machine precision) - The system is ill-conditioned

Iterative methods are preferred when: - n > 10,000 with sparse A (< 1% nonzeros) - Only moderate accuracy is needed (relative residual ~ 10^(-6)) - Memory is limited - The matrix has good spectral properties or effective preconditioners exist

3.11.2 Computational Complexity Analysis

For an n × n system with nnz nonzeros:

Direct methods (Sparse LU): - Setup cost: O(nnz^(4/3)) to O(n^3) operations (depends on fill-in) - Solve cost: O(n^2) operations per right-hand side - Memory: O(n^2) for factors (can be much larger than nnz)

Iterative methods: - Cost per iteration: O(nnz) for matrix-vector products - Total cost: O(k × nnz) where k is iteration count - Memory: O(nnz + kn) for method storage

The crossover point typically occurs around n = 10,000-100,000, depending on sparsity and conditioning.

Example 6.3 (Method Selection). Consider solving the 2D Poisson equation -∇²u = f on a 100×100 grid. This yields a 10,000×10,000 system with 5 nonzeros per row (50,000 total).

  • Direct: LU factorization creates significant fill-in, requiring ~10^8 operations and ~400MB storage
  • Iterative: CG with multigrid preconditioning converges in ~20 iterations, requiring ~10^6 operations and ~2MB storage

The iterative approach is 100× faster and uses 200× less memory.

function benchmark_methods()
    println("Benchmarking iterative vs direct methods")
    println("="^50)
    
    # Test different problem sizes
    for n in [100, 500, 1000]
        # Create symmetric sparse matrix (tridiagonal + perturbation)
        A = spdiagm(-1 => -ones(n-1), 0 => 3*ones(n), 1 => -ones(n-1))
        A += 0.1 * sprandn(n, n, 0.01)  # Add sparse random perturbation
        A = (A + A') / 2  # Ensure symmetry
        b = randn(n)
        
        println("\nProblem size: $(n)×$(n), nnz = $(nnz(A))")
        
        # Direct method timing
        t_direct = @elapsed x_direct = A \ b
        
        # Iterative method timing  
        t_iter = @elapsed begin
            x_iter, _ = conjugate_gradient(A, b, tol=1e-10)
        end
        
        println("Direct method:    $(round(t_direct*1000, digits=2)) ms")
        println("CG method:        $(round(t_iter*1000, digits=2)) ms")
        println("Speedup:          $(round(t_direct/t


\newpage

# Chapter 7: Eigenvalue Problems

The eigenvalue problem Ax = λx stands as one of the most fundamental problems in numerical linear algebra, with applications spanning virtually every field of quantitative science. From understanding the stability of dynamical systems through spectral analysis to dimensionality reduction via principal component analysis, eigenvalue computations form the backbone of countless algorithms in scientific computing.

This chapter develops the principal algorithms for computing eigenvalues and eigenvectors of matrices, beginning with classical power iteration methods and progressing through the sophisticated QR algorithm that underlies modern eigensolvers. We examine specialized techniques for symmetric matrices that exploit their favorable spectral properties, and conclude with the singular value decomposition, which extends eigenvalue concepts to non-square matrices.

## The Eigenvalue Problem

> **Definition 7.1 (Eigenvalue Problem).** Given an n×n matrix A, the eigenvalue problem seeks scalars λ (eigenvalues) and nonzero vectors x (eigenvectors) such that Ax = λx. The set of all eigenvalues forms the spectrum σ(A).

The characteristic polynomial det(A - λI) = 0 provides the theoretical foundation for eigenvalue computation, but direct polynomial root-finding proves numerically unstable for n ≥ 5. Modern algorithms instead employ iterative methods that avoid explicit characteristic polynomial formation.

> **Theorem 7.1 (Spectral Properties).** For an n×n matrix A:
> 1. A has exactly n eigenvalues (counting multiplicities) in ℂ
> 2. If A is real, complex eigenvalues occur in conjugate pairs
> 3. If A is symmetric, all eigenvalues are real
> 4. The spectral radius ρ(A) = max{|λ| : λ ∈ σ(A)} satisfies ρ(A) ≤ ||A|| for any induced matrix norm

*Proof.* Properties 1-2 follow from the fundamental theorem of algebra applied to the characteristic polynomial. Property 3 uses the fact that for symmetric A and eigenvector x, λ||x||² = x*Ax = (Ax)*x = λ*||x||², implying λ = λ*. For property 4, if Ax = λx with ||x|| = 1, then |λ| = ||λx|| = ||Ax|| ≤ ||A||||x|| = ||A||. □

## Power Method and Inverse Iteration

### The Power Method

The power method represents the conceptually simplest eigenvalue algorithm, computing the dominant eigenvalue (largest in magnitude) through repeated matrix-vector multiplication.

> **Algorithm 7.1 (Power Method).** Given matrix A and initial vector x₀:
> 1. For k = 0, 1, 2, ...
> 2. Set y_{k+1} = Ax_k
> 3. Set x_{k+1} = y_{k+1}/||y_{k+1}||
> 4. Set λ_{k+1} = x_{k+1}*Ax_{k+1}

The convergence analysis reveals the method's limitations and strengths.

> **Theorem 7.2 (Power Method Convergence).** Suppose A has eigenvalues |λ₁| > |λ₂| ≥ ... ≥ |λₙ| with corresponding eigenvectors v₁, ..., vₙ forming a basis. If x₀ = ∑cᵢvᵢ with c₁ ≠ 0, then
> λₖ → λ₁ and xₖ → ±v₁ with error O((|λ₂|/|λ₁|)ᵏ)

*Proof.* We have Aᵏx₀ = ∑cᵢλᵢᵏvᵢ = λ₁ᵏ(c₁v₁ + ∑_{i=2}ⁿ cᵢ(λᵢ/λ₁)ᵏvᵢ). Since |λᵢ/λ₁| < 1 for i ≥ 2, the sum converges to c₁v₁. Normalization at each step preserves this convergence pattern. □

The convergence rate depends crucially on the eigenvalue separation ratio |λ₂|/|λ₁|. When eigenvalues cluster near the spectral radius, convergence becomes prohibitively slow.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra

function power_method(A, x0; tol=1e-10, max_iter=1000)
    """
    Power method for computing dominant eigenvalue and eigenvector.
    
    Returns: (eigenvalue, eigenvector, iterations, residual_history)
    """
    x = copy(x0)
    λ = 0.0
    residuals = Float64[]
    
    for k in 1:max_iter
        # Power iteration step
        y = A * x
        x_new = y / norm(y)
        λ_new = dot(x_new, A * x_new)  # Rayleigh quotient
        
        # Check convergence
        residual = norm(A * x_new - λ_new * x_new)
        push!(residuals, residual)
        
        if residual < tol
            return λ_new, x_new, k, residuals
        end
        
        x = x_new
        λ = λ_new
    end
    
    @warn "Power method failed to converge in $max_iter iterations"
    return λ, x, max_iter, residuals
end

# Example: Compute dominant eigenvalue of a random matrix
n = 100
A = randn(n, n)
A = A + A'  # Make symmetric for real eigenvalues
x0 = randn(n)

λ_computed, v_computed, iters, residuals = power_method(A, x0)

# Compare with Julia's built-in eigensolver
eigenvals = eigvals(A)
λ_exact = maximum(eigenvals)

println("Computed dominant eigenvalue: $λ_computed")
println("Exact dominant eigenvalue: $λ_exact")
println("Error: $(abs(λ_computed - λ_exact))")
println("Iterations: $iters")

3.11.3 Inverse Iteration

Inverse iteration accelerates convergence to any specified eigenvalue by applying the power method to (A - σI)⁻¹, which amplifies eigenvalues closest to the shift σ.

Algorithm 7.2 (Inverse Iteration). Given matrix A, shift σ, and initial vector x₀: 1. Factor A - σI = LU (once) 2. For k = 0, 1, 2, … 3. Solve (A - σI)y_{k+1} = x_k via forward/backward substitution 4. Set x_{k+1} = y_{k+1}/||y_{k+1}||

When σ approximates an eigenvalue λᵢ, the method converges to λᵢ with rate O(|(λⱼ - σ)/(λᵢ - σ)|ᵏ) where λⱼ is the second-closest eigenvalue to σ.

function inverse_iteration(A, σ; x0=nothing, tol=1e-12, max_iter=100)
    """
    Inverse iteration for eigenvalue closest to shift σ.
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    if x0 === nothing
        x0 = randn(n)
    end
    
    # Factor A - σI once
    try
        F = lu(A - σ*I)
    catch
        error("Matrix A - σI is singular; σ is an eigenvalue")
    end
    
    x = copy(x0)
    λ = σ
    
    for k in 1:max_iter
        # Solve (A - σI)y = x
        y = F \ x
        x_new = y / norm(y)
        
        # Rayleigh quotient for eigenvalue estimate
        λ_new = dot(x_new, A * x_new)
        
        # Check convergence
        residual = norm(A * x_new - λ_new * x_new)
        if residual < tol
            return λ_new, x_new, k
        end
        
        x = x_new
        λ = λ_new
    end
    
    @warn "Inverse iteration failed to converge"
    return λ, x, max_iter
end

Example 7.1 (Vibrating String Eigenvalues). Consider the discrete second derivative operator arising from finite difference discretization of -d²/dx² on [0,1] with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions:

function tridiagonal_laplacian(n)
    """
    Discrete negative Laplacian matrix for vibrating string.
    Eigenvalues are 4sin²(kπ/(2(n+1))) for k=1,...,n
    """
    h = 1.0 / (n + 1)
    main_diag = fill(2.0/h^2, n)
    off_diag = fill(-1.0/h^2, n-1)
    return SymTridiagonal(main_diag, off_diag)
end

n = 50
A = tridiagonal_laplacian(n)

# Compute smallest eigenvalue (fundamental frequency)
h = 1.0 / (n + 1)
σ = 0.5 * 4 * sin(π*h/2)^2 / h^2  # Approximation to λ₁

λ₁, v₁, iters = inverse_iteration(A, σ)

# Exact eigenvalue
λ₁_exact = 4 * sin(π/(2*(n+1)))^2 / h^2

println("Computed λ₁: $λ₁")
println("Exact λ₁: $λ₁_exact")
println("Error: $(abs(λ₁ - λ₁_exact))")
println("Convergence in $iters iterations")

3.12 The QR Algorithm

The QR algorithm represents the gold standard for dense eigenvalue computation, simultaneously computing all eigenvalues through a sequence of orthogonal similarity transformations.

3.12.1 Basic QR Iteration

Algorithm 7.3 (Basic QR Algorithm). Given matrix A₀ = A: 1. For k = 0, 1, 2, … 2. Compute QR factorization Aₖ = QₖRₖ 3. Set Aₖ₊₁ = RₖQₖ

The fundamental insight is that Aₖ₊₁ = QₖᵀAₖQₖ, so all iterates remain similar to A and hence share its eigenvalues. Under suitable conditions, Aₖ converges to upper triangular form with eigenvalues on the diagonal.

Theorem 7.3 (QR Algorithm Convergence). If A has eigenvalues |λ₁| > |λ₂| > … > |λₙ| > 0 and is diagonalizable, then Aₖ converges to upper triangular form with diagonal entries λ₁, …, λₙ.

Proof Sketch. The QR algorithm implicitly performs simultaneous iteration on all canonical basis vectors. The orthogonalization process causes convergence to the Schur form. □

3.12.2 Shifts and Deflation

Convergence acceleration requires sophisticated shift strategies. The most effective approach uses the Wilkinson shift: the eigenvalue of the trailing 2×2 submatrix closest to the (n,n) entry.

function wilkinson_shift(A)
    """
    Compute Wilkinson shift for QR algorithm.
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    if n == 1
        return A[1,1]
    end
    
    # Extract trailing 2x2 submatrix
    a, b = A[n-1, n-1], A[n-1, n]
    c, d = A[n, n-1], A[n, n]
    
    # Eigenvalues of 2x2 matrix
    trace = a + d
    det = a*d - b*c
    discriminant = trace^2 - 4*det
    
    if discriminant >= 0
        sqrt_disc = sqrt(discriminant)
        λ₁ = (trace + sqrt_disc) / 2
        λ₂ = (trace - sqrt_disc) / 2
    else
        λ₁ = complex(trace/2, sqrt(-discriminant)/2)
        λ₂ = complex(trace/2, -sqrt(-discriminant)/2)
    end
    
    # Return eigenvalue closest to A[n,n]
    return abs(λ₁ - d) < abs(λ₂ - d) ? λ₁ : λ₂
end

function qr_algorithm_basic(A; tol=1e-12, max_iter=1000)
    """
    QR algorithm with Wilkinson shifts for eigenvalue computation.
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    A_work = copy(A)
    eigenvalues = ComplexF64[]
    
    while size(A_work, 1) > 1
        converged = false
        
        for iter in 1:max_iter
            # Check for convergence (subdiagonal element small)
            m = size(A_work, 1)
            if m == 1
                push!(eigenvalues, A_work[1,1])
                converged = true
                break
            end
            
            if abs(A_work[m, m-1]) < tol * (abs(A_work[m-1, m-1]) + abs(A_work[m, m]))
                # Converged: deflate
                push!(eigenvalues, A_work[m, m])
                A_work = A_work[1:m-1, 1:m-1]
                converged = true
                break
            end
            
            # Apply Wilkinson shift
            σ = wilkinson_shift(A_work)
            
            # QR step with shift
            Q, R = qr(A_work - σ*I)
            A_work = R*Q + σ*I
        end
        
        if !converged
            @warn "QR algorithm failed to converge"
            break
        end
    end
    
    if size(A_work, 1) == 1
        push!(eigenvalues, A_work[1,1])
    end
    
    return eigenvalues
end

3.12.3 Hessenberg Reduction

Direct application of the QR algorithm to general matrices proves inefficient due to the O(n³) cost per iteration. Preliminary reduction to upper Hessenberg form (zeros below the first subdiagonal) reduces this to O(n²) per iteration while preserving eigenvalues.

Algorithm 7.4 (Hessenberg Reduction). Use Householder reflections to reduce A to upper Hessenberg form H = Q^T A Q where Q is orthogonal.

function hessenberg_reduction(A)
    """
    Reduce matrix to upper Hessenberg form using Householder reflections.
    Returns (H, Q) where H = Q'AQ is upper Hessenberg.
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    H = copy(A)
    Q = Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)
    
    for k in 1:n-2
        # Extract column below diagonal
        x = H[k+1:n, k]
        
        if norm(x) < eps()
            continue  # Already in Hessenberg form in this column
        end
        
        # Construct Householder reflector
        e₁ = zeros(length(x))
        e₁[1] = 1.0
        
        α = sign(x[1]) * norm(x)
        v = x + α * e₁
        v = v / norm(v)
        
        # Apply to relevant submatrix
        # H[k+1:n, k:n] = H[k+1:n, k:n] - 2v(v'H[k+1:n, k:n])
        H[k+1:n, k:n] -= 2 * v * (v' * H[k+1:n, k:n])
        
        # H[1:n, k+1:n] = H[1:n, k+1:n] - 2(H[1:n, k+1:n]v)v'
        H[1:n, k+1:n] -= 2 * (H[1:n, k+1:n] * v) * v'
        
        # Update Q matrix
        Q[1:n, k+1:n] -= 2 * (Q[1:n, k+1:n] * v) * v'
    end
    
    return H, Q
end

3.12.4 The Complete QR Algorithm

Modern implementations combine Hessenberg reduction, implicit QR iterations with sophisticated shift strategies, and deflation techniques.

Example 7.2 (QR Algorithm Implementation). The following implements a simplified but functional QR eigensolver:

function qr_eigensolver(A; tol=1e-12)
    """
    Complete QR algorithm for eigenvalue computation.
    """
    # Step 1: Reduce to Hessenberg form
    H, Q = hessenberg_reduction(A)
    
    # Step 2: Apply QR algorithm to Hessenberg matrix
    eigenvals = qr_algorithm_basic(H; tol=tol)
    
    return eigenvals
end

# Test on a known matrix
A = [4.0 1.0 -1.0;
     1.0 3.0  0.0;
    -1.0 0.0  2.0]

computed_eigenvals = qr_eigensolver(A)
exact_eigenvals = eigvals(A)

println("Computed eigenvalues: ", sort(real(computed_eigenvals), rev=true))
println("Exact eigenvalues: ", sort(exact_eigenvals, rev=true))

3.13 Symmetric Eigenvalue Problems

Symmetric matrices possess remarkable properties that enable specialized, highly efficient algorithms. All eigenvalues are real, eigenvectors are orthogonal, and the spectral theorem guarantees diagonalizability.

Theorem 7.4 (Spectral Theorem). Every real symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as A = QΛQ^T where Q is orthogonal and Λ is diagonal with real entries (the eigenvalues).

3.13.1 Jacobi Method

The Jacobi method predates the QR algorithm but remains valuable for its simplicity and guaranteed convergence for symmetric matrices.

Algorithm 7.5 (Jacobi Method). Repeatedly apply plane rotations to annihilate off-diagonal elements: 1. While ||off-diag(A)|| > tolerance 2. Choose largest off-diagonal element A_{pq} 3. Compute rotation angle θ to annihilate A_{pq} 4. Apply rotation: A ← J_{pq}^T A J_{pq}

The rotation angle satisfies tan(2θ) = 2A_{pq}/(A_{pp} - A_{qq}).

function jacobi_rotation(A, p, q)
    """
    Compute Jacobi rotation to annihilate A[p,q].
    Returns rotation matrix J and updated matrix.
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    
    if abs(A[p, q]) < eps()
        return I(n), A  # Already zero
    end
    
    # Compute rotation angle
    if abs(A[p,p] - A[q,q]) < eps()
        θ = π/4
    else
        τ = (A[q,q] - A[p,p]) / (2*A[p,q])
        t = sign(τ) / (abs(τ) + sqrt(1 + τ^2))  # Smaller root
        θ = atan(t)
    end
    
    c, s = cos(θ), sin(θ)
    
    # Construct rotation matrix
    J = Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)
    J[p,p] = c;  J[p,q] = -s
    J[q,p] = s;  J[q,q] = c
    
    # Apply rotation
    A_new = J' * A * J
    
    return J, A_new
end

function jacobi_eigenvalue(A; tol=1e-12, max_iter=1000)
    """
    Jacobi method for symmetric eigenvalue problem.
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    A_work = copy(A)
    V = Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)  # Accumulate eigenvectors
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # Find largest off-diagonal element
        max_val = 0.0
        p, q = 1, 2
        
        for i in 1:n, j in i+1:n
            if abs(A_work[i,j]) > max_val
                max_val = abs(A_work[i,j])
                p, q = i, j
            end
        end
        
        if max_val < tol
            # Converged
            eigenvals = [A_work[i,i] for i in 1:n]
            return eigenvals, V
        end
        
        # Apply Jacobi rotation
        J, A_work = jacobi_rotation(A_work, p, q)
        V = V * J  # Accumulate eigenvectors
    end
    
    @warn "Jacobi method failed to converge"
    eigenvals = [A_work[i,i] for i in 1:n]
    return eigenvals, V
end

3.13.2 Divide-and-Conquer Algorithm

For large symmetric tridiagonal matrices, divide-and-conquer algorithms achieve O(n²) complexity by recursively splitting the problem and cleverly merging solutions.

The key insight partitions a tridiagonal matrix T as:

T = [T₁ 0 ] + [0 0 ] [0 T₂] [0 T₃]

where T₃ contains only the coupling terms between blocks.

3.14 Singular Value Decomposition

The singular value decomposition extends eigenvalue concepts to rectangular matrices and provides the foundation for numerous applications in data analysis and numerical methods.

Definition 7.2 (Singular Value Decomposition). For any m×n matrix A, there exist orthogonal matrices U ∈ ℝ^{m×m} and V ∈ ℝ^{n×n} such that A = UΣV^T where Σ is m×n diagonal with non-negative diagonal entries σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ … ≥ σₘᵢₙ ≥ 0 (the singular values).

The SVD connects intimately to eigenvalue problems: the singular values are square roots of eigenvalues of A^T A, while V contains the eigenvectors of A^T A and U contains the eigenvectors of AA^T.

3.14.1 Computing the SVD

The standard algorithm for SVD computation proceeds through bidiagonalization followed by iterative diagonalization:

  1. Bidiagonalization: Use Householder reflections to reduce A to bidiagonal form B = U₁^T A V₁
  2. Diagonalization: Apply variant of QR algorithm to B^T B or implicit QR to B itself
function power_method_svd(A; tol=1e-10)
    """
    Compute largest singular value and vectors using power method on A'A.
    This is for illustration; production codes use more sophisticated methods.
    """
    ATA = A' * A
    n = size(ATA, 1)
    v = randn(n)
    v = v / norm(v)
    
    for iter in 1:1000
        v_new = ATA * v
        σ² = dot(v, v_new)  # Rayleigh quotient
        v_new = v_new / norm(v_new)
        
        if norm(v_new - v) < tol
            σ = sqrt(σ²)
            u = A * v_new / σ
            return σ, u, v_new
        end
        v = v_new
    end
    
    error("SVD power method failed to converge")
end

# Example: SVD of random matrix
m, n = 50, 30
A = randn(m, n)

# Our simple method for largest singular value
σ₁, u₁, v₁ = power_method_svd(A)

# Compare with Julia's built-in SVD
U, Σ, V = svd(A)

println("Computed largest singular value: $σ₁")
println("Exact largest singular value: $(Σ[1])")
println("Error: $(abs(σ₁ - Σ[1]))")

3.15 Applications in Scientific Computing

3.15.1 Stability Analysis of Dynamical Systems

The stability of equilibrium points in dynamical systems dx/dt = f(x) depends on eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix ∂f/∂x evaluated at the equilibrium.

Example 7.3 (Predator-Prey Stability). Consider the Lotka-Volterra system:

dx/dt = αx - βxy dy/dt = δxy - γy

The Jacobian at equilibrium (γ/δ, α/β) has eigenvalues ±i√(αγ), indicating neutral stability.

using ForwardDiff

function lotka_volterra(u, p)
    x, y = u
    α, β, γ, δ = p
    return [α*x - β*x*y, δ*x*y - γ*y]
end

# Parameters and equilibrium point
params = [1.0, 0.5, 0.75, 0.25]  # α, β, γ, δ
x_eq = params[3] / params[4]  # γ/δ
y_eq = params[1] / params[2]  # α/β
equilibrium = [x_eq, y_eq]

# Compute Jacobian using automatic differentiation
J = ForwardDiff.jacobian(u -> lotka_volterra(u, params), equilibrium)
eigenvals = eigvals(J)

println("Equilibrium point: $equilibrium")
println("Jacobian eigenvalues: $eigenvals")
println("System stability: ", all(real.(eigenvals) .< 0) ? "Stable" : "Unstable/Neutral")

# [FIGURE: Phase portrait showing closed orbits around the equilibrium point]

3.15.2 Principal Component Analysis

PCA reduces dimensionality by projecting data onto eigenvectors of the covariance matrix corresponding to largest eigenvalues.

function pca_analysis(X; n_components=2)
    """
    Principal Component Analysis via eigenvalue decomposition.
    X: data matrix (observations × features)
    """
    # Center the data
    X_centered = X .- mean(X, dims=1)
    
    # Compute covariance matrix
    C = (X_centered' * X_centered) / (size(X, 1) - 1)
    
    # Eigenvalue decomposition
    eigenvals, eigenvecs = eigen(C)
    
    # Sort by decreasing eigenvalue
    idx = sortperm(eigenvals, rev=true)
    eigenvals = eigenvals[idx]
    eigenvecs = eigenvecs[:, idx]
    
    # Select top components
    principal_components = eigenvecs[:, 1:n_components]
    explained_variance = eigenvals[1:n_components] / sum(eigenvals)
    
    # Project data
    X_projected = X_centered * principal_components
    
    return X_projected, principal_components, explained_variance
end

# Generate sample data (3D ellipsoid)
n_samples = 200
θ = range(0, 2π, length=n_samples)
φ = range(0, π, length=n_samples÷2)

X = zeros(n_samples, 3)
for i in 1:n_samples
    # Ellipsoid with different axis lengths
    X[i, 1] = 3 * cos(θ[i]) * sin(φ[mod(i-1, length(φ))+1]) + 0.1*randn()
    X[i, 2] = 2 * sin(θ[i]) * sin(φ[mod(i-1, length(φ))+1]) + 0.1*randn()
    X[i, 3] = 1 * cos(φ[mod(i-1, length(φ))+1]) + 0.1*randn()
end

# Apply PCA
X_pca, components, var_explained = pca_analysis(X, n_components=2)

println("Explained variance ratios: $var_explained")
println("Total variance explained: $(sum(var_explained))")

3.15.3 Google PageRank Algorithm

PageRank computes the stationary distribution of a random walk on the web graph, equivalent to finding the dominant eigenvector of a modified adjacency matrix.

function pagerank(A; damping=0.85, tol=1e-8, max_iter=1000)
    """
    Compute PageRank scores using power iteration.
    A: adjacency matrix (A[i,j] = 1 if page j links to page i)
    """
    n = size(A, 1)
    
    # Normalize columns (outgoing links)
    col_sums = vec(sum(A, dims=1))
    for j in 1:n
        if col_sums[j] > 0
            A[:, j] ./= col_sums[j]
        else
            A[:, j] .= 1.0 / n  # Handle dangling nodes
        end
    end
    
    # Google matrix: M = d*A + (1-d)/n * ones(n,n)
    # We implement this implicitly to save memory
    
    # Power iteration
    x = ones(n) / n  # Initial uniform distribution
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        x_new = damping * (A * x) + (1 - damping) / n
        
        if norm(x_new - x, 1) < tol
            return x_new / sum(x_new), iter
        end
        
        x = x_new
    end
    
    @warn "PageRank failed to converge"
    return x, max_iter
end

# Example: Small web graph
# Pages: 1->2, 1->3, 2->3, 3->1, 3->2
A = [0 0 1;
     1 0 1;  
     1 1 0]

scores, iterations = pagerank(A)
println("PageRank scores: $scores")
println("Converged in $iterations iterations")

3.16 Advanced Topics and Implementation Notes

3.16.1 Julia’s Built-in Eigensolvers

Julia’s LinearAlgebra package provides highly optimized eigensolvers through LAPACK:

using LinearAlgebra

# Dense eigenvalue problems
A = randn(100, 100)
A = A + A'  # Make symmetric

# Complete eigendecomposition
eigenvals, eigenvecs = eigen(A)

# Only eigenvalues
λ = eigvals(A)

# Subset of eigenvalues/vectors for symmetric matrices
using Arpack
λ_subset, v_subset = eigs(A, nev=5, which=:LR)  # 5 largest real eigenvalues

# Generalized eigenvalue problem Ax = λBx
B = randn(100, 100); B = B'B + I  # Make SPD
λ_gen = eigvals(A, B)

# Singular value decomposition
A_rect = randn(50, 30)
U, σ, V = svd(A_rect)

3.16.2 Sparse Eigenvalue Problems

Large-scale applications require specialized methods for sparse matrices:

using SparseArrays, Arpack

# Create sparse tridiagonal matrix
n = 1000
A = spdiagm(-1 => fill(-1.0, n-1), 0 => fill(2.0, n), 1 => fill(-1.0, n-1))

# Compute smallest eigenvalues


\newpage

# Chapter 8: Numerical Methods for Initial Value ODEs

Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are ubiquitous in mathematical modeling across all scientific disciplines. From population dynamics to quantum mechanics, from financial models to climate simulations, the ability to solve initial value problems (IVPs) numerically is fundamental to scientific computing. While analytical solutions exist for only a narrow class of ODEs, numerical methods provide a powerful framework for approximating solutions to virtually any well-posed initial value problem.

Consider the general first-order IVP:

y’(t) = f(t, y(t)), t ∈ [t₀, T] y(t₀) = y₀


where f: ℝ × ℝᵈ → ℝᵈ is assumed to satisfy sufficient regularity conditions (typically Lipschitz continuity in y) to guarantee existence and uniqueness of solutions. Higher-order ODEs can always be converted to first-order systems by introducing auxiliary variables, making this formulation completely general.

The fundamental challenge in numerical ODE solving is discretization: we seek to approximate the continuous solution y(t) at a finite set of time points t₀ < t₁ < ... < tₙ = T. The key insight is that differential equations encode local information about the solution—the derivative at each point—and numerical methods exploit this to step forward in time.

## Euler's Method: The Foundation

The simplest and most intuitive numerical method for ODEs is Euler's method, which directly implements the geometric interpretation of the derivative as the slope of the tangent line.

> **Definition 8.1 (Forward Euler Method).** Given the IVP y' = f(t,y), y(t₀) = y₀, the forward Euler method with step size h produces the sequence:
> 
> yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + hf(tₙ, yₙ),  tₙ₊₁ = tₙ + h

The forward Euler method approximates the solution at tₙ₊₁ by following the tangent line from (tₙ, yₙ) for a distance h. This is geometrically intuitive but introduces truncation error at each step.

> **Theorem 8.1 (Local Truncation Error of Forward Euler).** If y ∈ C²[t₀, T], then the local truncation error of forward Euler satisfies:
>
> τₙ₊₁ = y(tₙ₊₁) - (y(tₙ) + hf(tₙ, y(tₙ))) = O(h²)

*Proof.* By Taylor expansion around tₙ:

y(tₙ₊₁) = y(tₙ) + hy’(tₙ) + (h²/2)y’‘(tₙ) + O(h³) = y(tₙ) + hf(tₙ, y(tₙ)) + (h²/2)y’’(tₙ) + O(h³)

Therefore τₙ₊₁ = (h²/2)y''(tₙ) + O(h³) = O(h²). □

The global error—the cumulative effect of local errors—is typically O(h), making forward Euler a first-order method.

### Backward Euler Method

While forward Euler uses the derivative at the current point to step forward, backward Euler uses the derivative at the next point:

> **Definition 8.2 (Backward Euler Method).** The backward Euler method produces:
>
> yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + hf(tₙ₊₁, yₙ₊₁)

This is an implicit method—we must solve a (possibly nonlinear) equation for yₙ₊₁ at each step. While computationally more expensive, backward Euler has superior stability properties that make it essential for certain classes of problems.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff

function forward_euler(f, y0, tspan, h)
    t0, tf = tspan
    t = t0:h:tf
    n = length(t)
    y = zeros(length(y0), n)
    y[:, 1] = y0
    
    for i in 1:n-1
        y[:, i+1] = y[:, i] + h * f(t[i], y[:, i])
    end
    
    return t, y
end

function backward_euler(f, y0, tspan, h; tol=1e-12, max_iter=50)
    t0, tf = tspan
    t = t0:h:tf
    n = length(t)
    y = zeros(length(y0), n)
    y[:, 1] = y0
    
    for i in 1:n-1
        # Newton's method to solve: y_{n+1} - y_n - h*f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1}) = 0
        y_new = y[:, i]  # Initial guess
        
        for iter in 1:max_iter
            residual = y_new - y[:, i] - h * f(t[i+1], y_new)
            
            if norm(residual) < tol
                break
            end
            
            # Jacobian: I - h * ∂f/∂y
            J = I - h * ForwardDiff.jacobian(y -> f(t[i+1], y), y_new)
            y_new = y_new - J \ residual
        end
        
        y[:, i+1] = y_new
    end
    
    return t, y
end

# Example: Exponential decay y' = -λy, y(0) = 1
λ = 2.0
f(t, y) = -λ * y
y0 = [1.0]
tspan = (0.0, 2.0)
h = 0.1

t_fe, y_fe = forward_euler(f, y0, tspan, h)
t_be, y_be = backward_euler(f, y0, tspan, h)

# Exact solution for comparison
y_exact = exp.(-λ * t_fe)

println("Forward Euler error at t=2: ", abs(y_fe[1, end] - exp(-2λ)))
println("Backward Euler error at t=2: ", abs(y_be[1, end] - exp(-2λ)))

3.17 Runge-Kutta Methods

While Euler’s method uses only the derivative at one point per step, Runge-Kutta methods achieve higher accuracy by evaluating the derivative at multiple points within each step interval.

3.17.1 Second-Order Runge-Kutta

The classical second-order Runge-Kutta method (also known as the midpoint method or Heun’s method) can be derived by attempting to match more terms in the Taylor expansion:

Definition 8.3 (Classical Second-Order Runge-Kutta).

k₁ = hf(tₙ, yₙ)
k₂ = hf(tₙ + h/2, yₙ + k₁/2)
yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + k₂

This method evaluates f at the midpoint of the interval [tₙ, tₙ₊₁], using the Euler prediction to estimate the solution value there.

3.17.2 Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta

The classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method (RK4) is perhaps the most famous ODE solver:

Definition 8.4 (Classical Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta).

k₁ = hf(tₙ, yₙ)
k₂ = hf(tₙ + h/2, yₙ + k₁/2)
k₃ = hf(tₙ + h/2, yₙ + k₂/2)
k₄ = hf(tₙ + h, yₙ + k₃)
yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + (k₁ + 2k₂ + 2k₃ + k₄)/6

The weights (1, 2, 2, 1)/6 arise from Simpson’s rule for numerical integration, reflecting the connection between ODE solving and quadrature.

3.17.3 Order Conditions and Derivation

The derivation of Runge-Kutta methods relies on matching Taylor expansions. For a general s-stage explicit Runge-Kutta method:

k₁ = hf(tₙ, yₙ)
k₂ = hf(tₙ + c₂h, yₙ + a₂₁k₁)
⋮
kₛ = hf(tₙ + cₛh, yₙ + aₛ₁k₁ + ... + aₛ,ₛ₋₁kₛ₋₁)
yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + b₁k₁ + ... + bₛkₛ

The coefficients {aᵢⱼ}, {bᵢ}, and {cᵢ} must satisfy increasingly complex order conditions to achieve higher accuracy.

Theorem 8.2 (Order Conditions for Runge-Kutta Methods). For order p accuracy, the Runge-Kutta coefficients must satisfy a system of nonlinear equations. For p ≤ 4, the minimum number of stages is s = p, but for p ≥ 5, more stages than the order are required.

The proof involves expanding both the numerical solution and the exact solution in terms of elementary differentials and matching coefficients—a calculation that becomes prohibitively complex for high orders.

function rk4_step(f, t, y, h)
    k1 = h * f(t, y)
    k2 = h * f(t + h/2, y + k1/2)
    k3 = h * f(t + h/2, y + k2/2)
    k4 = h * f(t + h, y + k3)
    return y + (k1 + 2*k2 + 2*k3 + k4) / 6
end

function rk4_solve(f, y0, tspan, h)
    t0, tf = tspan
    t = t0:h:tf
    n = length(t)
    y = zeros(length(y0), n)
    y[:, 1] = y0
